•Neovascular glaucoma is a Frequent sequelae to
anterior segment neovascularizalion.

- _Fund us examination_ may reveal:

 - _Venous dilatation_ with irregular calibre but no or
only mild tortuosity.

 - _Retinal arlerial narrowing_ is present.

 - _Retina shows_ mid peripheral dot and blot haemorrhages, microaneurysms and cotton wool spots.

 - _Retinal neouascularizalion_ is noted in 37% cases,
which may be in the form ofNVD and occasionally
NYE.

 - _Macular oedema_ is a common complication.

**Differential diagnosis.** Ocular ischaemic syndrome
needs to be differentiated from non-ischaemic CRVO,
diabetic retinopathy, hypertensive retinopathy and
aortic arch disease caused by Takayasu arteritis,
aonoarteritis, atherosclerosis and syphilis (Table 12.3).

**Investigations.** fn suspected cases, the carotid stenosis
can be confirmed by Doppler ultrasound and
magnetic resonance angiography.

**Treatment** of ocular ischaemic syndrome includes:

- _Trealment of neovascular glaucoma (See_ page 257).

- _Treatment of proliferaliue retinopathy_ by panretinal
photocoagu.lation (PRP) _(See_ page 29 1).

- _Treatment of pseudoiritis_ with topical steroid eye
drops.

- _Treatmentofcarolidstenosisis_ medical (antiplatelet
therapy, oral anlicoagulants) and surgical (carotid
endarterectomy).

## Dystrophies And Degenerations

## Of Retina

## Retinal Dystrophies

Hereditary retinal dystrophies primarily affect the
outer retina (photoreceptors and RPE). Common
retinal dystrophies can be classified as below:

**296** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

**Table 12.3** _Differential diagnosis of ocular ischaemic syndrome_

_**Condition**_

1. Nonischaemic Unilateral retinal haemorrhages, venous

CRVO dilatation and cotton wool spots

I _**Differences**_

Veins are more tortuous, haemorrhages are more
numerous, normal retinal arteriolar perfusion, disc
oedema, and sometimes optociliary shunt vessels
can be seen on the disc

Usually bilateral, with characteristic hard exudates

2. Diabet ic

retinopathy

3. Hypertensive

retinopathy

4. Aortic arch

disease

Microaneurysms, dot and blot
haemorrhages, venous dilatation, NVD and
NVE, cotton wool spots

Arteriolar narrowing and focal constriction, Usually bilateral, no marked venous changes, and
retinal haemorrhages and cotton wool spots NVD and NVE

Arteriolar narrowing, retinal haemorrhages,
cotton wool spots, NVD and NVE

Usually bilateral, absent arm and neck pulses, cold
hands, and spasm of arm muscles with exercise

**A. Generalised photoreceptor dystrophies**
These involve the entire retina (peripheral retina

more than macula) and include:

- Typical relinilis pigmentosa and its variants,

- Progressive cone dystrophy,

- Leber congenital amaurosis,

- Congenital stationary night blindness, and

- Congenital monochromatism (achromatopsia).

**B. Macular dystrophies**
These primarily involve the macular area and a few

common macular dystrophies are:

- Juvenile Best's macular dystrophy,

- Stargardt's disease, and

- Vi telliform dystrophy.

**Note.** Only the most important retinal dystrophy, the

retinitis pigrnentosa and its variants, are described here.

## Retinitis Pigmentosa

Retinitis pigmentosa, primary pigmenrary retinal
dystrophy is a hereditary disorder predominantly
affecting the photoreceptor (rods more than the cones).

**Inheritance**
Retinitis pigmentosa (RP) may occur as:

I. _Sporadic disorder(40-50%),_ isolated without family
hiscory due to mutation of multiple gene including
rhodopsin gene such cases _( unknown: simplex)_ have
more favo urable prognosis with retained central
vision till 6th decade of life.
2. _T 11/ierited d_ _is order_ as:

_• Autosomal dominant RP_ (adRP), common (1525%), least severe •..vith best prognosis

_• Autosomal recessive_ _RP_ (arRP ), less common
(5-20%), intermediate severity

- _X-linked recessive_ (XLR), least common (5-15% ), most
severe. X-linked inheritance is further of two types

 - simplex and multiplex. _Simplex_ reflects isolated
cases with one affected member. In _multiflex,_ at
least two family members are affected. Most of the
X-linked patients become blind by third decade.

- _Digenic inheritance_ is very rare.

**Pre:valence and demography**

- _Prevalence._ It occurs in l person per 5,000 of the
world population.

- _Age._ It appears in the childhood and progresses
slowly, often resulting in blindness in advanced
middle age.

- _Race._ No race is known to be exempt or prone to it.

- _Siex._ Males are more commonly affected than
females in a ratio of3:2.

- _Laterality._ Disease is almost invariably bilateral and
b,olh eyes are equally affected.

**Pathogenesis**
As a group, majority of retinitis pigrnentosa conditions
are characterized by _death of rod photoreceptors._ The
_molecular mechanism_ by which the genetic mutation
eventually causes rod ceU death are unclear, although
ample evidence indicates that apoptosis is involved
in th e final pathway of cell d eath. That the _cone_
_plwtoreceptors_ ultimately die from a disease that
begins with rod-cell disease remains a puzzle.

**Clil~ic-lnvestigative features**
Typical retinitis pigmentosa, i.e. rod-cone dystrophy,
in which rods are degenerated early and cones are
involved late. Depending on the relative involvement
of rods and cones photoreceptors there are two types
of retinitis pigmentosa.
_1ype 1 RP._ Patients display _early and preferential loss_
_of rod sensitivity_ and early signs such as diminished
nig,ht vision. This condition also progresses slowly and
leads to a region specific vision loss.
_Type 2 RP._ Patients have a progressive and _combined_
_loss of rods and cones sensitivity_ with onset in
adulthood and later onset of diminished night vision.

**A.** _**Visual symptoms**_
1. _Night blindness._ It is Lhe characteristic and earliest

feature and may present several years before the
visible changes in the retina appear. it occurs due
to degeneration of the rods.

2. _Dark adaptation._ Light threshold of the peripheral

retina is increased; though the process of dark
adaptation itself is not affected unlil very late.
3. _Tubular vision,_ i.e. loss of peripheral vision vvith

preservation of central vision occurs in advanced
cases.
4. _Central vision_ is also lost ultimately after many

years.

**B.** _**F,mdus changes**_ (Fig. 12.18)
l. _Retinal pigmentary changes._ These are typically

perivascular (around veins) and jet black spots
resembling bone corpuscles in shape. lnitially, these
changes are found in the equatorial region only and
later spread both anteriorly and posteriorly.
2. _Retinal arterioles_ are attenuated (narrowed) and

may become thread-like in late stages.
3. _Thinning_ _and atrophy_ of retinal pigment

epitheliwn (RPE) is seen in mid and far peripheral
retina with relative sparing ofRPE at the macula.
4. _Optic disc_ becomes pale and waxy in later stages

and ultimately consecutive optic atrophy occurs
(Fig. 12.18).
5. _Other associated changes_ which may be seen are

colloid bodies, choroidal sclerosis, cystoid macular
oedema, atrophic or cellophane maculopathy.

**C.** _**Visual field cha11ges**_
_Annular or ring-shaped scotoma_ (Fig. 12.19) is a

typical feature which corresponds to the degenerated
equatorial zone of retina. As the disease progresses,
scotoma increases anteriorly and posteriorly and
ultimately only central vision is left _(tubular vision)._

_consecutive optic atrophy_

Eventually, even this is also lost and the patient
becomes blind.

**D.** _**Electroiphysiological changes**_
Typical electrophysiological changes appear early in
the diseas,e before the subjective symptoms or the
objective signs (fund us changes) appear.

**l.** _Electroretinogram_ (ERG) is initially subnormal

(scotopicaffected beforephotopic; b-waveaffected
before ;a wave) and eventually extinguished.
2. _Electro-oculogram_ {EOG) is subnormal with an

absence of light peak.

**Associatio,ns of retinitis pigmentosa1.** _OculL1ra!;sociatio11s._ These include myopia, primary
open-ang,le glaucoma, microphthalmos, conical
cornea {keratoconus) and posterior subcapsular
cataract.
**11.** _Systemic a sso ciation s._ Most cases of retinitis

pigmentosa (RP) are isolated, i.e. with no systemic
features, such cases are labelled as _'retinitis_
_pigmentosa non-syndromic_ or _simple'._ About 25%
have associated systemic diseases and such cases
are labell1ed as _'syndromic retinitis pigmentosa'._ A
number of specific syndromes are described:

l. _Lauren.ce-Moon-Biedlsyndrome._ le is characterised

by re tinitis pigmentosa, obesity, hypogenitalism,
polydactyly and mental deficiency.
2. _Cockayne's_ _syndrome._ It comprises retinitis

pigme1ntosa, progressive infantile deafness,
dwarfism, mental retardation, nystagmus and,
ataxia.
3. _Re/sum's syndrome._ It is characterised by retinitis

pigmemosa, peripheral neuropachy and cerebellar
ataxia.

**298** SECTION Il l DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

4. _Usher's syndrome._ It includes retinitis pigmentosa

and labyrinthine deafness. Usher syndrome is of
three rypes:

  - _Type_ _1._ Congenital, profo und deafness with
vestibular dysfunction, extinguished ERG in
first decade.

  - _Type 2._ Congenital, moderate to severe deafness
with normal vestibular fu nction, vision loss in
_second decade._

   - _Type 3._ Progressive hearing loss and normal
dysfunction and relatively late onset pigmentary
retinopathy.
5. _Hallgren's syndrome._ lt comprises reti nitis

pigmentosa, vestibuJocerebellar ataxia, congenital
deafness and mental deficiency.
6. _Other associated syndromes include_ Bassen
Kornzweig syndrome (Abetalipoproteinaemia),
Kearns-Sayre syndrome, Friedreich's ataxia, BardetBiedJ syndrom e, NARP (neuropathy, ataxia, and
retinitis pigmentosa), neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis,
and olivopontocerebellar degeneration.

**Atypical forms of retinitis pigmentosa**
_Retinitis pigme11tosasi11e plgme11to._ It is characterised by
all the clinical features of typical retinitis pigmentosa,
except that there are no visible pigmentary changes
in the fundus.
_Sectorial retinitis pigme11tosa._ It is characterized by

involvement of only one sector of the retina.
_Perice11tric retinitis pigmer1tosa._ In this condition, all
the clinical features are similar to typical retinitis
pigmentosa except that pigmentary changes are
confined to an area, immediately around the macula.
_**Retir1itis punctata a**_ _**lbescens.**_ It is characterised by
the presence of innum erable discrete white dots
scattered over th e fundus witho ut pigmentary
changes. Other features are narrowing of arterioles,
night blindness and consrriction of visual fields.

**TreatmentIt is** most unsatisfactory; rather we can say that till
date there is no effective treatment for the disease.

**1.** _**Measures to stop progression,**_ which have been tried
from time to tim e, without any breakthrough include:
vasodilato rs, placental extracts, transp lantation
of rectus muscles in to su prach oroidal space,
light exclusion therapy, ultraso nic therapy a nd
acupuncture therapy.
_**RecentlyvitamhiA**_ _(15000 IV,_ PO, qd ofpalmitateform)
has been recommended to check its progression.
**2.** _**Correct any refractive error,**_ prescribe glasses.

**3.** _**Systemic acetazolamide**_ (500 mg po) for associated
cystoid macular oedema.

4. _Stem cell therapy_ for retinitis pigmentosa, still under

trial, is as below

- _Bone marrow derived_ mononuclear stem cells
are injected intravitreally and their role as _rescue_
_therapy_ is being evaluated.

- _Embryonic stem cells,_ _induced pluripotent stem_
_cells_ and _retinal progenitor cells_ are injected in
the subretinal space and their role as _replacement_
_therapy_ is being evaluated.
5. _Gene therapy trials,_ may end up in some fruitful
outcome.
6. _Retinal prosthesis (e.g. Bionic eye)_ may be of help for

blind patients with retinitis pigmentosa.
7. _Low vision aids (LVA)_ in the form of 'magnifying
glasses' and 'night vision device' may be of some
help.
a. _Rehabilitation_ of the patient should be earned out
as per his socioeconomic backgroun d.
9. _Prophylaxis._ Geneti c counselling for no Consanguineous marriages may help to reduce the incidence
of disease. Further, affected individuals should be
advised not to produce children.

## Retinal Degenerations

Retinal degenerations are acquired disorders of
retina characterized by degenerative changes. These
can be classified as below:

- Peripheral retinal degenerations,
•Vitreore Linal degenerati ons, and

- Macular degenerations, e.g.

 - Age-related degeneration _(See_ page 305), and

 - Myopic macular degeneration _(See_ page 41).

## Peripheral Retinal Degenerations

1. _Lattice degeneration._ Tt is the m ost important
degeneration Lhat predisposes to retinal detachment.
_Incidence_ is 6 to I 0% in general population and 15 to
20% in myopic patients being bilateral in 50% of cases.
_Characteristicfeature_ includes:

_While arborizing lines arranged in a lattice pattern_
along with areas of retinal thinning and abnormal
pigmentation (Fig. 12.20A).

 - _Small round retinal holes_ are frequently present in it.

 - _Typical lesion_ is spindle-shaped, located between
the ora serrata and the equator with its long axis
being circumferentially oriented.

- Involves more frequently the temporal than the
nasal, and the superior Lhan the inferior halves of
thefundus.
2. _Snail tract degeneration._ lt is a variant precursor of

lattice degeneration in which white lines are replaced
by snow-flake areas which give the retina a white
frost-like appearance (Fig. 12.20B).

_A._ _Lattice degeneration; B, Snail_ _track degeneration;_
_C, Acquired retinoschisis; D. White-with-pressure; E. Focal_
_pigment clumps: F, Diffuse chonoretinal degeneration:_ G,

_Peripheral cystoid degeneration_

3. _Degenerative reti11oschisis._ The term _relinoschisis_
refers lo splitting of the sensory retina into two layers
at the level of the inner nuclear and outer plex:iform
layers. It occurs in rwo forms-the congenital and
acquired called as degenerative.

_Degenerative retinoschisis_ is also called as _senile_
_retinoschisis,_ may rarely act as predisposing factor for
primary retinal detachment. The condition occurs in
about 4% of population and is frequently bilateral.
Retinoschisis occur in clinically indistinguishable
two forms - typical (retinal splitting in the outer
plexifonn layer) a nd reticular (retinal splitting in
nerve fibre layer. Reticular degenerative retinoschisis
may be predisposed to retinal detachment due
to hole formation a nd progressive extension:
and not the typical, which is usually localized. It
is characterised by thin, transparent, immobile,
sha ll ow e levation of the inn er retinal layers
(Fig. l2.20C) which typically produces absolute field
detects-the fact which helps in differentiating it
from the shallow retinal detachment which produces
a relative scotoma. It usua lly involves the inferotemporal quadrant, anterior to the eq uator followed
by superotemporal quadrant.
**4.** _**White-w ith-pressure and wh**_ _**ite-without pressure.**_
These are not uncommonly associated \-vith retinal
detachment.

- _'White-with-pressure'_ lesions are characterised by
greyish Lranslucent appearance of retina seen on
scleral indentation (Fig. l 2.20D).

- _'White-without-pressure'_ lesions are located in the
peripheral retina and may be associated with lattice
degeneration.

5. _Focnl pigment clumps._ These are small, localised
areas of irregular pigmentation, usually seen in
the equatorial region ( Fig. 12.20E). These may be
associated with posterior vitreous detachment and/
or retinal tear.
6. _Peripheral chorioreti11al_ _atrophy (paving stone_
_dege11erntion)._ It is characterised by diffuse areas of
retinal thinning and depigmentation of underlying
choro id (Fig. 12.20F). lt occurs in about one-third of
adult eyes and is thought to occur due to choroidal
vascular insufl:iciency. The lesions appear as isolated
or grouped, small discrete yellow -white _areas_ with
pigm e nted borders and promi ne m underlyi ng
choroidal vessels.
7. _Microcystoid retinal degeneration._ It is a common
degen eration (Fig. 12.20G) seen as bubbles or
vacuoles in the peripheral retina of old people that
may be confused with retinal holes. It may preruspose
to retinal detachment in some very old people.

## Vitreoretinal Degenerations

ViLreoretinal degenerations or vi treoretinopathies
include:

- Wagner's syndrome,

- Stickler syndrom e,

- Favre-Goldmann syndrome,

- Familial exudative virreoretinopathy,

- Erosive vitreoretinopathy,

- Dominant neovascular inflammatory vitreoretinopathy, and

- Dominant vitreoretinochoroidopathy.

**Note.** Characteristic features of some conditions arc

mentioned here.

**Wagner's syndrome**
Wagner's syndrome has an autosomal dom inant
(AD) inheritance with fo llowing features:

- _Vitreous_ is liquified with condensed membranes.

- _Retina_ shows narrow and sheathed vessels, and
pigmented spots in the periphery.

- _Choroid_ may be atrophied.

- _Cataract_ may develop as late complication.

**Stickler syndrome**
Stickler syndrome, also known as _hereditary arthro-_
_ophthalmopathy,_ is an a utosomal dominant
conncclivc tissue rusorder characterized by following
features:

I Ocular features are as below:
_Vitreous_ is liquified and shows syneresis giving
appearance of an optically-em pry viueous caviry.
_Progressive myopia_ is very common.
_Radial lattice like degeneration_ associated with
pigmcnta ry changes and vascular sheathing.

**300** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

_Bilateral retinal detachmen t_ may occur in 30% cases
( commonest inherited cause of retinal detachment
in children).
_Ectopia le11tis_ is occasionally associated.

_Pre-senile cataract_ occurs in 50% cases.
II **Orofaclal abnormalities** include flattened nasal
bridge, maxillary hypoplasia, cleft palate and high
arched palate.

**111Arthropathy** is characterized by stiff, painful,
prominent and hyperextensible large joints.
Other features include deafness and mitral valve
prolapse.

**Favre-Goldmann syndrome**
It is an autosomal recessive condition presenting in
childhood with nyctalopia.
_**Characteristicfeat11res**_ are:

- _Vilreous_ shows syn eresis but the cavity is not
optically empty.

- _Relinoschisis,_ both central (affecting macula) and
peripheral, is present, although macular findings
are more subtle.

- _Pigmentary changes_ similar to retinitis pigmencosa
are marked.

- _ERG_ is subnormal.

## Macular Disorders

Macula, being concerned with cenual vision, has
attracted the atlention of many retina specialists.
Consequently, many disorders have been defined
and variously classified. A simple, _etiological_
_classification_ for a broad overview of the macular
lesions is as follows:
**A.** _**Congenital anomalies.**_ These include ap lasia,
hypoplasia and coloboma.
B. _Heredilarydystrophies._ These include Best's disease,
Stargardt's disease, butterfly-shaped dystrophy, bull's
eye dystrophy and central areolar dystrophy.
**C.** _**Acq11ired maculopathies**_ include:
1. _Traumatic lesions._ These include traumatic

macular oedema, traumatic macular degeneration,
macular haemorrhage and macular hole _(See_
page 449).
2. _Inflammations,_ i.e. central chorioretinitis _(See_

page 168).
3. _Solar retinopathy,_ i.e. photoretinitis.
4. _Degenerations._ Important conditions are age
related macular degeneration (ARMD), and
myopic degeneration.
5. _Metabolic disorders_ include: diabetic maculopathy

and sphingolipidosis.
6. _Toxic_ _maculopathies_ include: chloroquine and

phenothiazine-induced maculopathy.

7. _Miscellaneous acquired maculopathies._ A few

common conditions are:

  - Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR),

  - Cystoid macular oedema (CME),

  - Macular hole,

  - Macular epiretinal membrane,

  - Vitreomacu1ar traction syndrome, and

  - Idiopathic choroidal neovascularizarion.
**ote.** Only a few macular disorders are described.

## Hereditary Macular Dystrophies

Common heredomacu lar dystrophies can be
classified according to the anaromical level of retinal
involvement as below:

- _Inner retina:_ X-linked juvenile retinoschisis.

- _Photoreceptors:_ Cone-rod dystrophy

- _Retinal pigment epithelium:_ Best disease, Stargardt
disease and fundus flavimaculatus.

**X-linked juvenile retinosch isis**
X-linked juvenile retinoschisis is thought to be due
to Muller cell dysfunction and affects young males.
_**Characteristicfeatllres**_ include:

- _Gradual progressive loss of vision_ during first or
second decade of life; which may remain then
stable until the fifth or sixth decade.

- _Foveal scf1isis_ is the typical macular lesion, which
on slit-lamp examination is seen as stellate pattern
of small superficial retinal cysts with radial striae
centered on the fovea.

- _Peripheral retinoschisis_ may be present in 50% of
the cases.
_l1111esligalionswhich_ give useful information include:

- _Fundus fluorescein_ _angiography (FFA)_ shows
normal posterior pole and thus differentiates from
the mimicking cystoid macular edema (CME).

- _Electroretinogram(EEG)_ is negative, i.e. normal
amplitude of a wave with reduced b-wave
amplitude; typical of inner retinal disorders sparing
photoreceptors. ERG helps in differentiating it from
_enhanced S-cone (Goldmann_ _Favre) syndrome,_ in
which ERG is ex'tinguished.

**Cone-rod dystrophy**
Cone-rod dystrophy, also known as 'cone-dominant
dystrophy' is a comparatively rare heredomacular
dystrophy.

Inheritance. Most cases are sporadic but there may
be autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive and
x-linked inheritance.

**Characteristic features.** In this condition, cones are
degenerated earlier and more severely than rods. It
presents in early adulthood with following features:

_Symptoms_ include reduced central vision more
marked in bright light (Hemeralopia) associated with
defective colour vision.

_Fu11dus_ _exnmi11atio11_ may be normal in many
cases or may show some macular lesions. The
characteristic bilateral _'Bull's eye pattern'_ of macular
depigmentation is relatively uncommon, when
present needs to be differentiated from chloroquine
retinopathy.
_Frmdusjluorescein angiography(FFA)_ shows patchy
areas of hypo- and hyper-fluorescence. The typical
Bull's eye pattern lesion is characterized by a
zone of hyperfluorescence surrounding a central
nonfluorescent spot.
_Electroreti11ography_ shows a marked loss of cone
function and slight to moderate loss or rod function.
Treatment is nol known.

**Best's disease**

_**J11herite11ce:**_ AutosomaJ dominant
_Clinicc,l picture_ or _Best vitelliform macular dystrophy_
can be divided into five stages:

- _Pre-vitelliform stage._ Normal fundus, but EOG is
abnormal.

- _Vitelliform_ _stage._ Egg yolk lesion at macula
(Fig. 12.21).

- _Pseudohypopyon stage._ Panially absorbed egg yolk
lesion.

- _Vitelli eruptive stage._ A scrambled egg appearance
of macula.

- _Stage of scarring._ Hypertrophic or atrophic vascularized scar at macula.
_Electrophysiological tests._ Hallmark of Best disease is
normal ERG with abnormal EOG (electrooculogram).
OCT shows hyperreflective material beneath and
within the RPE.

_Fundus autofluorescence_ shows intense hyperautofluorescence of the yellowish lesions and
hypoautofluorescence of the atrophic area.

_(egg yolk lesion at macula)_

**Stargardt'!, disease**
Stargardt's disease, also known as _'juvenile macular_
_dystrophy;_ is by far the most common macular
dystrophy.

_Inherila11ce_ is usually aurosomal recessive but but
rarely auto,somal dominant is also known.
_Clinical presentation_ is with decreased vision in first
or second decade oflife.

_Fundris_ _examination_ initially is normal but
subsequently shows _'beaten-bronze'_ or _'snail-slime_

_reflex'_ in macular area.
_ERG._ The full field ERG is usually normal, but _pattern_
_ERG_ is completely extinguished, even when central
vision is good.

**Fundus flavimaculatus**
Fundus tlaivimaculatus is genetically considered to
be a part of the same spectrum as Stargardt's disease.
_Cliniwf pr,esentatio11_ is with reduced central vision
which is late than Stargardt's disease, i.e. usually

after 40 years of age.
_Fundus_ _shc,ws_ multiple yellowish-white retinal flecks
of variable size and shape distributed over whole of
the posteri.or pole.
_ERG_ changes in full field ERG may be seen even
before the vision is reduced and are important for
predicting prognosis.
_Treatment._ - specific treaunent is known. However,
Vitamin A supplementation should be avoided, as
increased levels of vitamin A are associated with
progression of the disease.

## Solar Retinopathy

_Solar reLinopathy_ also known as _photoretinitis,_ or
_eclipse retinopathy,_ refers Lo retinal injury induced
by direct o,r indirect sun viewing. Solar retinopathy
is associated with religious sun gazing, solar eclipse
observing, telescopic solar viewing, sun bathing and
sun watdting in psychiatric disorders.
_Causes of photic reti11opathy,_ other than solar
retinopathy, are:

- Welding.arc exposure,

- Lightening retinopathy, and

- Retinal pll10totoxicity from ophthalmic instruments
like operating microscope.

**Pathogem!sis**
Photoretinitis results from _photochemical reaction_
following _exposure of the retina to shorter wave-_
_length in_ _the 11isible spectrum,_ i.e. blue/violet-blue
light with a small contribution by UV-A rays (and
not by the infrared rays as was thought earlier).
Therefore, photoretinitis is also called as _blue-light_
_retinal injury._

**302** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

**Clinical features**
_Symptoms._ These include:

- _Persistence of negative after-image_ of the sun,
progressing later into a positive scotoma a nd
metamorphopsia.

_• Decreased_ _vision_ (6/12-6/ 60) ( unil ateral or
bilateral) which develops within 1 to 4 hours after
solar exposure, usually improves to 6/6-6/ I 2 v.rithin
six months.
_**Signs.**_ Initially, the fu ndus may appear normal.
Shortly after exposure a _small yellow spot_ with
grey margin may be noted in the foveolar and
parafoveo lar region. The _typical lesion,_ which
appears later, consists of a cenLral burnt-out hole in

the pigment epithelium surrounded by aggregation
of mottled pigme nt. Ophthalmoscopically, it
appears as a bean- or kidney-shaped pigmented
spot w ith yellowish white centre in the fo veal
region. In worst cases, typical macular hole may
appear.

**Treatment**
_No effective treatment_ is known for photo retinitis, so
emphasis should be on prevention. Eclipse viewing
should be discouraged unless there is proper use
of protective eye wear filters ( which absorb UV and
infrared wavelengths).
_Prognosis_ is guarded, since some scotoma and loss

in visual acuity by one or tvvo li nes mostly persists.

**CENTRAL SEROUS CHORIORETINOPATHY**
Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR) or also
known as central serous choroidopathy (CSC) or
earlier known as central serous retinopathy ( CSR) is
characterised by spontaneous serous detachment
of neurosensory retina in the macular region, with
or without retinal pigment epithelium detachment.

**Etiopathogenesis**
_**Riskfactors**_ include:

- _Age and sex._ The idiopathic disease affects typically
yo ung adult (20- 50 yea rs), males m ore than
females,

- _Personality._ Type A personality individuals are
more prone,

- _Steroid_ intake is an important risk factor,

- _Emotional stress, may also predispose_

- _Systemic lupus atheromatosis (SLE)_

- _Pregnancy_ ( usually 3rd trimester), and

- _Cushing's disease_ are also reported as risk factors.
_Patlzugen_ _es_ _is_ is n ot known exactly. Various
theori es have been postula ted. Presently, the
m ost acce pted theory is of' _clzoroidal_ _vascular_
_hyperpermeability'._ This theory correlates the

cliinical findings with indocyanine green (ICG)
angiography fmdings. It proposes that sympathetic
drive, sympathomimetics and co rticosteroids
alt1er the choroidal vascular permeability either
dinectly or indirectly by affecting its autoregulation.
This, in turn, increases the tissue hydrostatic
pressure in tl1e choroid causing pigment epithelial
defect (PED) resulting in a breach in the outer
blood retinal barrier. Leakage of fluid across this
area results in development of localized serous
detachmem of neurosensory retina.

**Clinical features**

_Symptoms._ Patient may present with:

- _Sudden painless loss of vision_ (6/9-6/24)

_• R_ _elative_ _positive_ _scotoma,_ _micropsia_ _and_
_metamorphopsia._

_Signs._ Biomicroscopic fundus examination reveals:

- _Mild elevation of macular area,_ demarcated by a
circular ring reflex.

- _Small yellow grey elevations_ may be seen due to
RPE detachment.

- _Foveal reflex_ is absent or distorted (Fig. 12.22).

- _Subrerinal deposits_ may be seen.

- _Multifocal_ pigmenrary changes suggest chronicity.

**Clinical course**
CSCR is usually self-limiting but often recurrent.
Three patterns are known:
_Acute classic CSCR_ is characterized by short clinical
co urse wi th sp ontan eo us resolu tion wi thin
3- fi months with near no rm a l visual recovery.
Recurrences are known in 30-50% of all the cases.
_Chronic CSCR,_ also termed as _diffuse retinal pigment_
_epitheliopathy_ (D RPE), is seen in few cases. lt is
characterized by a chronic course lasting more than

_central serous retinopathy_

12 months, typically affecting individuals above 50
years of age. Such cases may have permanent visual

impairment due to progressive RPE atrophy and
photoreceptor degeneration.
_Bullou.s CSCR_ is rare presentation characterized by

larger and more numerous areas of serous retinal
and RPE detachments often confused with bullous
retinal detachment.

**Investigations**
1. _Fu11du.sfl11oresceinangiography_ helps in confirming
the diagnosis. Two patterns of progressive leakage
and pooling from one or more points seen are:

 - _ink-blot pattern._ It consists of small hyperfluorescent
spot which gradually increases in size (Fig. 12.23A).

_•_ _Smoke-slack_ _pattern._ It consists of a small
hyperfluorescent spot which ascends vertically
like a smoke-stack and gradually spreads laterally
to take a _mushroom or umbrella configuration_
(Fig. 12.23B).
2. _JCG_ shows multiple areas ofhyperfluorescence due
to choroidal hyperpermeability.
3. _OCT_ shows neurosensory retinal detachment
and accompanying small detachments of the RPE
(Fig. 12.23C).

**Differential diagnosis**
Differential diagnosis of CSR includes other entities
which may produce serous detachment of sensory
retina in macular area. These include optic pit,
idiopathic polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy,
macular hole with serous detachment, choroidal
tumors and pigment epithelial defect (PED).

**Treatment**

I. _Conservative measures_ include:
_Reassurance_ is the only treatment required in majority
of the cases, since CSR undergoes spontaneous
resolution in 80 to 90% cases. Visual acuity returns
to normal or near normal within 1 to 6 months.
Only about 10- 20% cases do not have spontaneous
resolution. Approximately 20- 30% of cases will have
one or more recurrences.

- _Discontinuation of steroids,_ if possible, should be
done at the earliest.

- _Lifestyle changes_ to reduces stress in life, should
be adopted.
2 . _Las_ _er phowcoag ulalion_ is indicated in following
cases:

- Long-standing cases (more than 3 months).

- Patients having recurrent CSR with visual loss.

- Patients having permanent loss of vision in the
other eye due to this condition.
_Contraindications_ include the cases having leak
near or within the FAZ.

_pattern; A, and smoke-stack pattern; B, of hyperfluorescence_
_in central serous retinopathy; and C. OCT picture of CSR_

3. _Ph otody_ _namic_ _therapy (PDT)_ may be beneficial
for those with severe disease not a menable to
conventional laser treatment, e.g. with sub-foveal
leaks and chronic cases.

4. Anti-VEG1Fcan be considered if CNV develops.

## Cystoiid Macular Oedema (Cme)

It refers to,collection of fluid in the outer plexiform
(Henle's layer) and inner nuclear layer of the retina,
centred around the foveola.

**Etiology**
It is associated with a number of disorders. A few
common causes are as follows:

**304** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

1. _Complication of ocular treatment_ such as:

- _Ocular surgery,_ e.g. cataract extraction (Irvine-Gass
syndrome), penetrating keratoplasty, glaucoma

filteration surgery and retinal detachment surgery.

- _Ocular laser therapy,_ e.g. panretinal photocoagulation (PRP), and d:YAG laser capsulotomy.

- _Top_ _ical_ _ocular therapy_ with eye drops li ke
epinephrin e, dipivefrin e and prostaglandin
analogues, especially in patien ts who have
undergone cataract surgery.
2. _Retinal vascular disorders,_ e.g. diabe tic retinopathy
and central retinal vein occlusion (CRVO), branch
retinal vein occlusion (BRVO), retinal telangiectasia
(e.g. Coats' disease), periphlebi tis retinae (Eales
disease), and hypertensive retinopathy.
3. _llltraoculttr i11fla_ _mmalions1_ e.g. pars planitis,
posterior uveitis and anterior uveitis.
4. _Reti11al dystrophies,_ e.g. retinitis pigmentosa.
5. _Vitreomacular tractioll_ as seen in macular epiretinal
membra ne (ERM) and vitreomacular traction (VMT)
syndrome.
6. _Systemic diseases_ such as leukaemia, chronic renal

failure and multiple myeloma.
7. _Miscella,ieous causes_ include intraocular tumours,

choroidal neovascularization (CNV) and collagen
vascular diseases.

**Pathogenesis**
CME d evelops due to leakage of fluid following
_breakdown of inner blood-retinal barrier(i.e.1eakage_
from the retinal capillari es) and accumulating in the
outer plexiform and inner nuclear layer ofretina with
the formation of cyst-like cha nges as depicted on
OCT macular scan.

**Clinical features**

I . _Visual loss._ Initially, there is minimal to moderate
loss of vision, unassociated with other symptoms.
If oedema persists, there may occur permanent
decrease in vision.
2. _Opl1thalmoscopy_ reveals loss of foveal contour,
retinal thickening, a yellow spot at the center of fovea
and in clinically established cases a typical _'Honey-_
_comb appearance'_ of macula (due to multiple cystoid
oval spaces) (Fig. 12.24). CME is best examined with
a fundu s contact lens on slit-lamp or +900 lens.
Associated features depend on the underlying cause,

e.g. CRVO, etc.
3. _Fu11dus jluorescein_ _angiogra_ _phy_ demonstrates
leakage and accumulation of dye in the macular
region which in a well-established case presents a
_'.flower petal appearance'_ in late frames (Fig. 12.25A).

4 . _Optical coherence tomography (OCT)_ reveals loss
of foveal depression, intraretinal area of decreased

reflectivity with round optically clear regio ns
(cystoid spaces) and over all retinal th ickening
(Hg. 12.258). Detection race on OCT is similar to FFA.
Tc _can_ also detect associated specific pathology, e.g.
vitreomacular traction.

**Co,mplications**
Long-standing CME may end in lamellar macular
hole.

_appearance,n cysto,d macular oedema (CME)_

Fiog. 12.25 _Cystoid macular oedema:_ A, _Fundus fluorescein_
_ang,ogram showing flower petal appearance;_ _B,_ _OCT_
_picture showing cystoid spaces_

**Treatment**
I. _Treatment of the causatille factor,_ e.g. intravitreal
anti-VEGF or photocoagulation for diabetic CSME;
cessation of causative topical 2% adrenaline eye drops
and soon.
2. _Topical antiprostaglantl/11_ (NSAID) drops like
ketorolac, diclofenac or profenac when used preand postoperatively, prevent the occurrence of CME
associated with intraocular sw-gery. These are also
useful in the treatment of most CMEs when given
for 3 to 4 months.
3. _Topical and systemic steroids_ may be of some use in
established cases.
4. _Systemic carbonic a11hydmse i11l,ibitors (CAls),_ e.g.
oral acetazolamide may be beneficial in some cases
of CME, e.g. in retinitis pigmentosa.

**AGE-RELATED MACULARDEGENERATION**
Age-related macular degeneration (AMD), also
called _senile macular degeneration,_ is a bilateral
disease of persons over 50 years of age. It is a leading
cause of blindness in developed countries, in
population above the age of 65 years.

**Etiopathogenesis**
AMD is an age-related disease of worldwide prevalence.
Genetic and environmental factors have been
implicated in the pathogenesis.

_Genetic/actors._ Current evidence suggests genetic
susceptibility involving the complement pathway.
Twin studies and linkage analysis have identified
multiple loci for ARMD genes.
_Risk factors_ which may affect the age of onset and/or
progression include, nutrition, smoking, female gender
hypertension, e:Kposw-e to sun light, hyperopia, blue
eyes and cataract particularly nuclear opacity. The
disease is most prevalent in Caucasians.

**Classification of AMO**

Conventionally, ARMED is of two types non-exudative
and exudative.

l. _No11_ _-ex11datlve or arrophic ARMD (Fig._ _12.2GA):_ It
is also called _dry ARMD,_ _and_ is responsible for
90% cases.

2. _Exudative AMD:_ It is also called _wet_ or _neovascular_
_AMD_ (Fig. 12.26B). It is responsible for only 10%
cases of AMD but is associated with comparatively
rapidly progressive marked loss of vision.
_Macular Research Classification Committee (M RCC)_ has
recently given a consensus classification of ARMD
based on the presence of lesions within two disc
diameter of the fovea in either eye. Following stages
have been described:

_Stage 1_ - _No apparent ageing changes:_ - drusens
and no pigmentary changes of ARMD.
_Stage_ 2 - _Normal ageing changes:_ Only druplets
(newly pmposed term for small drusen <63 um in
diameter) and no pigmentary changes.
_Stage 3_ - _Early AMD:_ Medium drusens, (>63 um to

125 um in diameter) and no pigrnentary changes.
_Stage_ _4_ -- _Intermediate AMD:_ Large drusens
(> 125 um in diameter) and / or any pigmentary
changes of AMD.
_Stage 5_ - _Late AMD:_ Neovascular AMD and/ or any
geographic atrophy.

**Clinical feaituresSymptoms** _of AMD_ include:

- _Gradual/ progressive diminution of vision_ occurs in
geographic/atrophic or dry AMD.

- _Common_ _visual complaints_ are distorted vision,
seeing straight lines as-wavy, bent or fuzzy. These
are shadowed areas in the central visual field
(positive scotoma) causing difficulty in reading.

- _Sudden_ _painless loss of vision_ may occur in
neovascular AMD.

Signs of AM D include:
_Drnsens i11_ _AMD_ are well defined, yellowish -white
slightly elevated spots seen in the macular area
(Fig. 12.26A). These are formed due to e>..'tracellular
deposits between the RPE and Bruch membrane.
These are of three types:

- _Small drusens,_ now called as _druplets_ are <63 um
in diameter. Size of the drusen can be estimated by
comparison with the diameter of retina vein, which
is approximately 125 um in width. Druplets are sign
of normal ageing. Term hard drusen has also been
used for s,maller drusen.

_• Medium_ _drusens_ are 63-125 um in diameter.
Presence of medium drusens is sign of early AMD
and carry very little risk of progression to late AMD
over next 5 years.

- _Large drusens_ are >125 um (i.e. equal to width
of retina1 vein) in diameter. They are a sign of
_intermediate AMD._ Earlier, the term soft drusens
has also been used for large drusens. Large drusens
in both eyes are associated with a 13% risk of
progression to lace AMO in 5 years. But when
associated with bilateral pigrnemary changes, risk
rises to 50%.
_Pigme11tary abnormalities in AMD._ Pigmentary
abnormaliities alone or along with large drusen
may be se,en in the macular area in patients with
_intermediate stage of AMD._ Pigmentary changes
include ar,eas of hyperpigmentation (due to focal
clumps of pigment cells in the subretinal space and

**306** SECTI ON Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

anterior retina) and hypopigmentarion (due co RPE
atroph y).
_**Geographic/ atropltic AMD.**_ Geographic atrophy
refers to well demarcated areas, (greater Lhan 175
um in diameter), of progressive atrophy of RPE
a nd photoreceptor cells. ln the areas of atrophy
underlying larger choroidal vessels may become
visible and pre-existing drusens may disappear.
_Neovasc_ _ular_ _**AMO**_ is a type of late AMD and is
characterized by following lesions:

- _Drusens with retinal pigment epithelial detachment_
(PED) seen as sharply circumscribed dome- shaped
elevation.

- _Choroidal neovascularizalion_ (CNV) proliferating
in sub-RPE space (type 1) is seen as greyish green or
pinkish yellow raised lesion (Fig. 12.26B). The CNV
proliferaling in sub-retinal space (type 2) is seen as
sub-retinal halo or pigment plaque.

- _Haemorrhagic pigment epithelial_ _detachment_
_(PED)._ May occur which appears as dark elevated
mount.

- _Haemorrhagic detachment of neurosensory retina_
which assumes diffuse ouLline and a lighter red

colour around and adjacent to the PED, may also
occur.

- _Disciform sub-retinal scarring_ in the macular region
may result from gradual organization of blood with
further fibrovascular ingrowth.

**Diagnosis**
_Clinical diagnosis_ is made from the typical signs
d escri bed above, which are best elucidated on
examination of the macula by slit-lan1p biomicroscopy
with a +900 /+ 780 non-contact lens or Mainster
contact lens.

_**Fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) in AMD**_

_Drnse11,_ during the early phase of FFA show punctuate
hyperfluorescence, corresponding to window defects
due to ove rlying RPE atrophy. In the late phase, the
hyperlluorescence may decrease in smaller drusen

and increase in larger drusen.
_Geographic atrophy,_ in early phase of FFA is seen
as a well-de marcated window defect wi th a mild
increase of hyperfluorescence in late phase. On
ICGA, there may be moderate loss of choriocapillaris
with preservation of medium and large choroidal
vessels.
_**Choroidal neovascularization**_ (CNV) in relation

to foveal avascular zone is best detected by fundus
fluorescein angiography and indocyanine green
angiography CNV may be classical or occult:

- _Classical CNV_ is seen as lacy hyperlluorescence
with progressive leakage (Fig. 12.26C). lt may be
subfoveal, juxtafoveal or extrafoveal in location.

- _Occult CNV_ is seen as stippled hyperlluorescence
( type I) or as late leakage of undetermined source.
(type 11).
_On autojluorescence imaging,_ areas of geographic
atrophy shows hyperlluorescence.

_**Optical Coherence tomography (OCT)**_
**Drusen,** on OCT, are seen as hyperreflective irregular
nodules (bumps) located between the RPE and
Brucb's membrane.
Geographic atrophy, on OCT, is seen as severe
disruption or absence of the external limiting
membrane of retina.

**Neovascular AMO,** on OCT reveals subretinal fluid,
intrarednal thickening, choroidal neovascularization
and haemorrhages (Fig. 12.260).

**TreatmentA.** _**Prophylactic therapy to prevent progression to**_
_**lateAMD**_
I. _Vitamin and a11tioxida11t s11ppleme11tation._ Agerelated eye disease study 1 and 2 (AR EDS l & 2),
both have reported role of antioxidant supplements

in preventing progression to late AMD.
_Tndicalions_ include

- Early AMD (med ium d ru se n >63- 125 um in
diameter) in both eyes.

- Intermediate AMO (large drusen - 125 um in
diameter)

- Unilateral CNV
_Recommendations_ as per A REDS 2 (daily dose) are:

- Vitamin C: 500 mg

- Vitamin E: 400 mg

- Lutein: 10 mg

- Zeaxanthin: 12 mg

- Zinc: 25-50 mg (lower dose is equally effective)

- Copper: 2 mg ( this may not be required with lower
zinc dose).

I I. _**Othe r m e**_ _**asures**_ recommended to prevent
progression of AMO include:

 - _Cessation of smoking_ may slow the progress.

 - _Green leafy vegetables,_ to be used liberally.

 - _Protective measures against excessive sunlight_
exposure should be considered.

 - _Regular consumption of oily fish,_ may also be useful.

**B.** _**General measures**_

 - _Amsler grid_ used regularly allows the patients
to detect new or progressive metamorphopsia
prompting them to seek ophthalmic advice.

_exudative; B, Exudative; C, FFA showing leakage and lacy_
_pattem in CNV; D, OCT picture of CNV_

_Refraction_ with increased near add may be helpful

in early cases.

**C.** _**Treatment modalities available for neovascular**_
_**AMD:**_

I . _ln tra_ _vilreal anti-VEGF therapy_ has become the
treatment of first choice for all CNV lesions. AntiVEGFs are injected intravitreally. Th ese include:

- _Bevacizumab_ (Avastin), dose: 1.25 mg, or 0.05 mL

- _Ranibizumab_ (Lucentis ), dose: 0.5 mg/0.05 mL (For
detail _See_ page 478).

Results of anti-VEGF are encouraging in the sense
that they improve the vision in 30-40% of cases and
stabilize the vision in rest of Lhe cases. However, the
effect is short lasting and repeated injections are
required at. an interval of 1-3 monlhs. VEGF-trapeye (aflibercept), a fusion protein, is reported to be
equally effective as standard therapy, but requires
fewer injections into the eyes.
_Role ofanti-.PDGF therapy._ Pericytes are the source to
resistance to anti-VEGF therapy in neovascular AMD
Hence, there is a physiological rationale in combining
the anti-PDGF and anti-VEGF therapies. The anti PDGF agent strips p ericytes from neovascular
complex, rendering the CNV more vulne rable to
anti-VEGF treatment.

**ote.** Although anti-VEGF therapy is the preferred
strategy for the treaunent of CNY, some patients may
choose not to undergo an intravitreal procedure. ln
these cases, options include PD1: TPT, argon laser
photocoagiulation.
2. _Photody11a111 ic therapy (_ _PDT)_ is the treatment of choice
after anti-VEGFinjections for subfoveal and juxtafoveal
classic CNVM. In PDT, Verteporfin, a photosensitizcr
or light activated dye is injected intravenously. The
area of CNVM is then exposed to light from a diode
laser source at a wavelength (689 nm) that corresponds
to absorption peak of the dye. The light-activated
dye then causes disruption of cellular sn1.1ctures and
occlusion of CNVM with minimum damage to the
adjacent RPE, photoreceptors and capillaries.
3. _Tra,1sp11r,1illary thermotherapy (TTT)_ with a diode
laser (810 nm) may be considered for subfoveal
occult CNVM. PDT is definitely bener than 1Tf but
is very expensive.

4. _Doublefreque11cy YAG 532 rim_ _photocoagula_ _tion_
may be used fo r extrafoveal choroidal neovascular
membrane (C _NM)._
5. _Low vision aid_ may be needed in advanced cases of
geographical atrophy.
6. _Surgical treatment._ Submacular surgery to remove
CNVM and macular translocation surgery are being
evaluated.

**MACUU\R HOLE**
Macular hole refers to a partial thickness or fu ll
thickness hole in the neurosensory retina in the
foveal region.

**Causes**

1. _Senile_ or _idiopathic_ (83%), more common in

females aged 60-80 years than males (F:M, 3:1).
2. _Trauma.tic macular hole_ account for (5%) cases.

**308** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

3. _Other causes_ of macular hole include: cyscoid

macular oedema, vitreomacular traction, postsurgical, myopia, post-laser treatment, epiretinal
membrane traction and post-inflammatory.
_**Pathogenesis:**_ Senile macular holes are caused by
tractional forces associated with early PVD.

**Clinical featuresSymptoms** include:

- _Decreased vision,_ typically around 6/60 level for
a _full_ thickness hole and better for a partial hole.

- _Metamorphopsia_ or distortion of vision may be
there.

- _Central scotoma_ may be reported by some
patients.

Signs, are depicted with following examination
techniques:
_Amsler grid testing_ shows a central scotoma.

_Watzke-Allen test-_ A thin line oflight is projected over
the macula with a slit lamp and the patient is asked
co report on its appearance. A line appearing broken
may indicate a macular hole.
_FundusexaminatiotL_ Based on the fundus appearance
(best examined with 78/90D slit-lamp examination),
the macular hole can be classified into four stages
{Fig. 12.27):

_**Gass classification of macular hole**_
_Stage 1 or impending hole._ It is characterized by absent
foveal reflex and a yellow spot (Stage lA, Fig. 12.27A)

         - -' ... . _,.

_OCT pictures (B, D, F,_ HJ, _respectively_

or a yellow ring (Stage lB) in the foveal region.

_OCT examination_ reveals a pseudocyst with a
vitreous detachment in stage l A (Fig. 12.27B}, and
progression of the pseudocyst with a break in the
outer foveal layer in stage 18.
_**Stage 2.**_ A small full U1ickness hole (Fig. 12.27C) is
seen either in the centre of ilie ring (central) or at

the margin ofilie ring (eccentric). _OCT examination_
shows a full iliickness hole less than 400 ~Lm in size
(Fig. 12.270}.
_**Stage 3.**_ A full thickness hole is seen as round
reddish spot surrounded by a grey halo [cuff of subretinal fluid (SRF)], but no PVD (Fig. 12.27£). _OCT_
_examination_ shows a full thickness macular hole
more than 400 ~Lm in size with partial vitreomacular
adhesion/traction (Fig. 12.27F).
_**Stage 4.**_ Full thickness hole with SRF cuff and complete
PVD, i.e. posterior vitreous detachment (seen as Weiss
ring) (Fig. 12.27G). OCT _examination_ confirms a large
full thickness hole with posterior vitreous detached
from the disc and macula. (Fig. 12.27H).

**Management**
_**Differential diagnosis**_ of fundus appearance include:

- Macular pucker (epiretinal membrane) with
pseudohole,

- Solar retinopathy,

- lntraretinal cyst (e.g. chronic CME with prominent
central cyst) and

- Vitreomacular traction syndrome.

_**Investigations**_
1. _Fundus fl.uorescein angiography_ reveals early

foveal hyperfluorescence without leak in the late
phase in patients with stage 2 to 4 macular holes.
2. _Optical coherence tomography (OCT)_ (Fig. 12.27)

is useful for:

  - Differentiating true holes from the lamellar
holes and cysts,

  - Staging of tlle macular hole,

  - Determining the degree of traction from the
epiretinal membrane, and

  - Planning the surgery.
_**Treatment**_
_•Stage 1._ Treatment is nor recommended as
spontaneous hole closure can occur. But close
follow-up and observation is required as 50% cases
do progress.
_•Stage 2 to 4_ holes of recent onset (<l year) with
reduced visual acuity ( <6/ 24) should be treated
with pars plana vitrectomy with posterior hyaloid
removal, inLernal limiting membrane (TLM)
peeling and gas or silicon oil tamponade with su•icr
postoperative face down position for 7-14 days.

_Prognosis._ Anatomical closure is reported in 80-90%
of cases. Visual improvement is reported in 70% cases
with recent onset holes.
_Complicaticm s of surgery_ include:

- _Occurrence_ _or_ _progression_ _of cataract_ is a
comparatively common complication. Therefore,
even if early cataract is present, the cataract surgery
should be performed just before or at the time of
macular hole surgery.

- _Other reported complications_ include: retinal
breaks, retinal detachment, late reopening of the
hole, RPE loss under the hole, phototoxicity and
endophthalmitis.

## Etinal Detachment

Retinal de tacbment is the separation of neurosensory retiina proper from the pigment epithelium.

ormally, 1bese two layers are loosely attached to
each otherwitll a potential space in between. Hence,
actually sp1eak.ing the term retinal detachment is a
misnomer .and it should be retinal separation.

**Classificatii,n**
Clinico-etiologically retinal detachment can be
classified into three types:

I. Rhegmatogenous or primary retinal detachment,
2. Tractional retinal detachment, and
3. Exudative retinal detachment

**RHEGMAT4:>GENOUS OR PRIMARY RETINAL**

## Detachmient

Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment usually
associated with a retinal break (hole or tear) through
which subretinal fluid {SRF) seeps and separates me
sensory retina from the pigmentary epitllelium. This
is the commonest type of retinal detachment.

**Etiology**
Tt is still not clear exactly. The predisposing factors
and the proposed pathogenesis is as follows:
**A.** _**Predisposi11gfactors**_ include:
1. _Age._ The condition is most common in 40-60

years. However, age is no bar.
2. _Sex._ More common in males (M:F-3:2).
3. _Myopia._ About 40% cases of rhegmatogenous

retinal d,etachmenr are myopic.
4. _Aphakia: and pseudophakia._ The condition is

more common in aphakes and pseudophake than
phakes.
5. _Retinal degenerations_ predisposed to retinal

detachment are as follows:

  - Lattice degeneration,

  - Snail track degeneration,

  - White-with-pressure anti white-without-or
occult pressure,

## Etinal Detachment

**310** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

  - Acquired or degenerative retinoschisis, and

  - Focal pigment clumps.
6. _Trauma._ It may also act as a predisposing factor.
7. _Senile posterior vitreous detachment (PVD}._ It is

associated with retinal detachment in many cases.
**B.** _**Pathoge11esis**_
Pathogenesis of rhegmatogenous retinal detachment
(RRD) is summarized in Fig. 12.28. The relinal breaks
responsible for RHD are caused by the interplay
between the _dynamic vilreoretinal traction_ and
predisposing degeneration in the peripheral retina.

Dynamic vitreoretinaJ uaction is induced by rapid
eye movements especially in the presence of PVD,
vitreous syneresis, aphakia and myopia. _Once the_
_retinal break_ is formed, the liquified vitreous may
seep through it separating the sensory retina from
the pigment epithelium. As the subretinal fluid
(SRF) accumulates, it tends to gravitate downwards.
The final shape and position of RD is determined

by location of retinal break _(Linco.ff's rule)_ and the
anatomical limits of optic disc and ora serrata.

The degenerated fluid vitreous seeps through
the retinal break and collects as subretinal fluid
(SRF) between the sensory retina and pigmentary
epithelium.

**Clinical features**
_Prodromal symptoms_ include:

- _Dark spots_ (floaters) in front of the eye (due to rapid
vitreous degeneration), and

 - _Photopsia,_ i.e. sensation of flashes of light (due to
irritation of retina by vitreous movements).

Symptoms of detached retina are as follows:

l. _Localised relative loss_ _in the field of vision_ ( of

detached retina) is noticed by the patient in
early stage which progresses to a total loss when
peripheral detachment proceeds gradually towards
the macular area.

2. _Sudden appearance of a dark cloud or veil_ in front

of the eye is complained by the patients when the
detachment extends posterior to equator.
3. _Sudden painless loss of vision_ occurs when the

detachment is large and central.

Slg;ns. These are elicited on following examinations:

I . _External examination,_ eye is usually normal.

2. _l'ntraocular pressure_ is usually slightly lower or may
be normal.
3. _Marcus Gwm pupil_ (relative afferent pupillary
de.feet) is present in eyes with extensive RD.
4. _Pla11e_ _mirror examination_ or _Distant Direct_
ophthalmoscopy reveals an altered red reflex in the
pupillary area (i.e. greyish reflex in the quadrant of
detached retina).
5. _Ophtlwlmoscopy_ should be carried out both direct
and indirect techniques. Retinal de tachment, is
best examined by indirect ophthalmoscopy using
scleral indentation ( to enhance visualization of the
peripheral retina anterior to equator).

- _Freshly-detached retina_ gives grey reflex instead of
normal pink reflex and is raised anteriorly (convex
configuration). It is thrown into folds which oscillate
1,1,ith the movements of the eye. These may be small
or may assume the shape of balloons in large
bullous retinal detachment. In total detachment
r,etina becomes funnel-shaped, being attached only
at the disc and ora serrata. Retinal vessels appear as
dlark tortuous cords oscillating with the movement
of detached retina.

 - _Retinal breaks_ associated with rhegmatogenous
dletachment are located with difficulty. These look
reddish in colour and vary in shape. These may be
round, horse-shoe shaped, slit-like or in the form
of a large anterior dialysis (Fig. 12.29). Retinal
breaks are most frequently found in the periphery
(commonest in the upper temporal quadrant).
Associated retinal degenerations, pigmentation
amd haemorrhages may be discovered.

 - _Vitreous_ _pigments_ may be seen in the anterior
vitreous (tobacco dusting or _Shaffer sign)._ with
posterior vitreous detachment.

 - _Old retinal detachment_ is characterized by
retinal thinning (due to atrophy), formation of
s,ubretinal demarcation line (high water marks)
clue to proliferation of RPE cells at the junction
of flat detachment and formation of secondary
intraretinaJ cysts (in very old RD).
6. _Visual fieltl clwrtillg_ reveals scotom as corresponding to the area of detached retina, which are
relative to begin with but become absolute in longstanding cases.

posterior
vitreous
detachment
(acute PVD)
