1

The degenerated fluid vitreous seeps through the retinal
break and collects as subretinal fluid (SRF) between
the sensory retina and pigmentary epithelium.

1
Retinal detachment

_rhegmatogenous retinal detachment_

_B, Round retinal hole;_ C, _Anterior dialysis_

7. _Electroretinography (ERG)_ is subnormal or absent.
a. _Ultrasonography_ confirms the diagnosis. It is
of particular value in patients with hazy media
especially in the presence of dense cataracts and
vitreous haemorrhage.

**Complications**
Complications usually occur in long-standing cases
and include proliferative vitreoretinopathy (PVR),
complicated cataract, uveitis and phthisis bulbi.

**Treatment**

Basic principles and steps of RD surgery are sealing
of retinal breaks, reducing the vi rreous traction on
the retina, and flattening of retina by draining of
subretinaJ fluid and external or internal tamponade.

1. Sealing of relinal breaks. All the retinal breaks
s hould be de te cted, acc u rately localised and
sealed by producing aseptic chorioretinitis, with
cryocoagulation, or photocoagularion or diathermy.
CryocoaguJation (Fig. 12.30) is utilised, with scleral
buckling and pneumoretinopexy while endo-laser
photocoagulation is used during V-R surgery.

**2. Drainageof SRP.** It allows immediate apposition
between sensory retina and RPE. SRF drainage is
done very carefully by inserting a fine needle through
the sclera and choroid into the subretinal space and
allowing SRF to drain away. SRF drainage may not
be required in some cases.

3. Ma inte nan ce of chorior e tina l a pposition is

required for at least a couple of weeks. This can be
accomplished by either of the following procedures
depending upon the clinical condition of the eye:
i. _Sciera{ buckling,_ i.e. inward indentation of sclera
to provide _external tamponade_ is still widely used
to achieve the above mentioned goal successfully
in simple cases of primary RD. Sciera! buckling is
achieved by inserting an explant (silicone sponge
or solid silicone band) with the help of mattress
type sut ures ap plied in the sclera (Fig. 12.31).

Rad ially-o riented explant is most effective in
sealing an isolated hole, and ci rcumferential explant
(encirclagie) is indicated in breaks involving three or
more quadrants.
ii. _Pneumatic relinopexy_ is a s imple out-patient
procedure which can be used to fix a fresh superior

np temperalure -70°C
(freezing the tissue
on contact)

Pressure under

direct vision

_direct vision with indirect ophthalmoscopy_

_subre/inal fluid (SRF) drainage_

**312** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

RD with one or rwo small holes extending over less
than two clock hour area in the upper two-thirds
of peripheral retina. ln this technique after sealing

the breaks wilh cryopex:y, an expanding gas bubble
(SF6 or C3 F8 ) is injected in the vitreous. Then proper
positioning of the patient is done so that the break
is uppermost and the gas bubble remains in contact
wilh the tear for 5-7 days.
ill. _Pars plan,, vitrectomy, endolaser plwlocoagulation_
_a11d internal tamponade._ This procedure is indicated in:

- All complicated primary RDs, and

- All lractional RDs.

- Presently, even in uncomplicated primary RDs
(where scleral buckling is successful), the primary
vitrectomy is being used with increasing frequency

by the experts in a bid to provide better results.

_Main steps_ oflhis procedure are:

- _Pars plana, 3-portvitrectomy (See_ page 269) is done
to remove all membranes and vitreous and to clean
the edges of retinal breaks.

- _Internal drainage of SRF_ through existing retinal
breaks using a fine needle or through a posterior
retinotomy is done.

- _Flallening of the retina_ is done by injecting silicone
oil or perlluorocarbon liquid.

- _Endolaser_ is then appl ied around the area of
posterior retinotomy, retinal tears, and holes to
create chorioretinal adhesions.

 - _To_ _tamponade the retina internally_ either silicone
oil is left inside or is exchanged with some longacti ng gas (gas-silicone oil exchange). Gases
commonl y used to tamponad e the retina are
sulphur hexalluoride (SF 6) or perfluoropropane
(C1 F8) _(See_ page 269).

**Prognosis**
Anatomical results of surgery are very good, i.e.
attachments of retina is achieved in most cases.
However, visual results depend on the pre-operative
status of the macuJa. lf the macula has been detached,

recovery of central vision is usually incomplete. Thus,
surgery should be performed urgently if the macula
is still not detached. Once the macula is detached,
delay in surgery for up to 1 week does not adversely
influence visual outcome.

**Prophylaxis**
Occurrence of primary retinal detachment can be
prevented by timely application of laser photocoaguJation or ciyotherapy in the areas of retinal breaks
and/or predisposing lesions like lattice degeneration.
Prophylactic measures are particularly indicated
in patients having associated high-risk factors like

myopia, aphakia, retinal detachment in the fellow eye
or histoiy of retinal detachment in the family.

## Exudative Or Solid Retinal Detachment

Exudat.ive (serous) retinal detachment occurs due
to lhe retina being pushed away by a neoplasm or
accumulation of fluid beneath the retina foll owing
inflam matory or vascular lesions.

**Etiology**
Common causes of exudative retinal detachment can
be grouped as under:

I. _Systemic diseases._ These include: toxaemia of
pregnancy, renal hypertension, blood dyscrasias and
polya rteritis nodosa.
2. _Ocular diseases._ These include:
i. _Congenital abnormalities_ such as nanophthalmos,

optic pit, choro idal coloboma and famil ial
exudative vitreoretinopathy (FEVR);
ii. _Jnflammaliuns_ s uch as Harada's disease,

sympathetic ophthalmia, posterior scleritis, and
orbital celJuJitis;
iii. _Vascular_ _diseases_ s uch as ce n tral serous

retinopathy and exudative retinopathy of Coats;
iv. _Neoplasms,_ e.g. malignant melanoma of choroid

retinoblastoma (exophytic type), haemangioma,
and metastatic tumours of choroid;
v. _Sudden hypotony_ due to perforation of globe and

intraocuJar operations.
vi. _Uveal effusion syndrom.e_ is cha racterised by

bilateral detachment of the peripheral choroid,
cilia1y body and retina.
vii. _Choroidal neovascu.larization_ may also cause

exudative retinal detachment.

**Clinical features**
Exudative retinal detachment can be differentiated
from a simple primary detachmen t by:

 - _Absence_ of photopsia, holes / tears, folds and
undulations.

 - _The exudatiue retinal detachm_ _ent_ is smooth and
convex (Fig. 12.32). At the summit of a tumour
it is usually rounded and fixed and may show
pigmenta1y disturbances.

 - _Pattern_ _of retinal_ _uessels_ m ay be di s turbed
occasionally, due to presence of neovascuJariza tion
on the tumour summit.

 - _Shifting.fluid_ characterised by changi ng position
of the detached area with gravity is the hallmark of
exudative retinal detachment.

 - _On transillumination test_ a simple detachment
appears transparent while solid detachment is
opaque.

**313**

_malignant melanoma of choroid_

**Investigations**

I. _Ocular and systemic examination_ s ho uld be

carried out thoroughly.
2. _B-scan ultrasonography_ may help delineale Lhe

underlying cause.
3. _PFA_ may show source offluid.
4. _CT scan_ and/ or MRI is useful, especially in cases

of intraocular tumours.

**Treatment**

_Treatment of the cause_ is required in most of the cases,
as the exudative retinal detachment due to transudate,
exudate and haemorrhage may undergo spomaneous
regression following absorption of the fluid. Thus, the
treatment should be for lhe causative disease.

 - _£nucleation_ is usually required in the presence of
intraocular tumours.

## Tractional Retinal Detachment

Tractional retinal detachment (TRD) occurs due to
retina being mechanica lly pulled away from its bed
by the contraction of fibrous tissue in the vitreous
(vitreoretinal u·acdonal bands).

**Etiology**
TRD is associaled with Lhe following conditions:

- Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (most common)

- Post- traumatic retraction of scar tissue especially
fo llowing penetrating injury,

- Post-haemorrhagic retinitis proliferans,

- Retinopathy of prematurity,

- Plastic eyelids,

- Sickle cell retinopathy,

- Proliferalive retinopathy in Eales' disease,

- Vitreomacular traction syndrome,

- Incontinenda pigmcnti,

- Retinal dysplasia, and

- Toxocariasis.

_advanced diabetic retinopathy_

**Clinical fea·tures**
Photopsia and floaLers are not complained. Tractional
retinal detaichment (Fig. 12.33) is characterised by:

 - _Presence of vitreorelinal bands_ wi th lesions of the
causative disease.

 - _Retinal breaks_ are usually absent.

 - _Configuration of the detached_ area is concave and
more locaLiized and usualily does not extend up to
ora serrata.

 - _Highest elevation of the retina_ occurs at sites of
vitreorelinal traction.

 - _Retinal mobility_ is severely reduced and shifting
fluid is absenL

 - _Focal_ _traction from_ _cellular membranes_ ca n
sometimes produce a retinal te ar an d lead to
a combined traction -rhegmatogenous retinal
detachment.

**Treatment**

_• Surgery_ iis difficult a nd req uires pars plana
vitrectomy to cue the vitreoretinal tractional bands
and intem aLI tamponade with either a long-acting
gas or silicon oil.

- _Prognosis_ in such cases is usually not so good.

## Tumours Of Retina

Tumours,of retina have become a subject of
increasing interest to clinical ophlhalmologists as

**A.** _**Primary tumours**_

1. _Neuroretlfnal tumors_

- Retinobla:sroma

- Astrocytoma

## Tumours Of Retina

Tumours,of retina have become a subject of
increasing interest to clinical ophlhalmologists as
well as ocular pathologists. Classification of retinal
tumours is given below and only a few, which arc
commonly seen are described.

**314** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE ANO OCULAR ADNEXA

2. _Retinal pigment epithelial(RPE) tumours_

- Congenital hypertrophy ofRPE

- Congenital simple hamartoma of RPE

- Combined hamartoma of retina and RPE

- Adenoma a nd adenocarcinoma of RPE

- Hyperplasia and migration ofRPE simulating uveal
melanoma
3. _Retinal vascular tumours_

- Capillary haemangioma

- Cavernous haemangioma

- Racem ose haemangioma

- Vasoproliferative tumor
4. _Primary vilreorelinal lymphoma._

**B.** _**Secondary tumours**_
l. _Direct extension,_ e.g. from malignan t melanoma

of the choroid.
2. _Metastatic carcinomas_ from the gastrointestinal

n·act, genitourinary Lract, lungs, and pancreas.
3. _Metastatic sarcomas._
4. _Metastatic malignant melanoma_ from the skin.

**C.** _**Para11eoplastic syndromes**_

L. Cancer-associated retinopathy
2. Melanoma-associated retinopathy.

## Neuroretinal Tumours

## Retinoblastoma

Retinoblastorna is a common malignant tumour arising
from the neurosensory retina in one or both eyes. After
uveal melanoma, it is the second mosl common
malignant intraocular tumor.

**Demographic data**

I. _Prevalence._ It is the most common inLraocular

tumour of c hildhood occurring 1 in 15,000 to
20,000 live births, and accounts for about 3% of
all childhood cancers.
2. _Age._ Hetinoblsatoma is co nfined to infancy and

very young children usually seen between 1 and
2 years of age.
_3._ _Sex._ There is no sex predisposition.
4. _Race._ It is rarer in Negroes than Whites.
5. _Laterality._ Retinobl astoma is unilateral in

70-75 of cases. In 25-30% cases, there is bilateral
involveme nt, although one eye is affected more
extensively and earlier than the othe r.

**Genetics and heredity**
Retinoblastoma gene (RB) h as been id~ntified as 14
band on the long a rm of chromosome 13 (l 3q 14)
and is a _'cancer suppressor' or 'antioncogenic' gene._

Deletion or inactivation of both the normal alleles
of this protective gene by two mulations _(Knudson's_
_two hit hypothesis, 1971)_ results in occ urrence of
retinoblastoma.

_Some facts about occurrence of retinoblastoma_ are as
bellow:
_•O,f all cases,_ only 10% are familial (inhe rited by
autosomal dominant m ode) and the rest about 90%
occur sporadically.

- _O,f sporadic cases,_ about rwo-chirds (i.e. 60% of a ll
cases) occur by somatic mutation and one-Lhird
(i.e. 30% of all cases) occur by germ line mutation.

- _T.he retinoblastoma lhus, occurs_ either as heritable
(germline) cases (40%) or n on-heritable somatic
cases (60%).

I . _**Heritable or germline cases.**_

- _First hit (mutation),_ in such cases occurs in one of
di e two alleles of retinoblastoma gene on the germ
c,elJs (gametes), before fertilization. This occurs in
40% of all cases eilher due to inheritance from the
affected parent (10% cases) or sporadically in one of
tl1e gametes (30% cases). This means mutation will
occur in all the somatic cells (predisposing to develop
even nonocular tumours such as osteosarcoma).

- _Second hit ( mutation}_ occurs late in the postzygotic
phase an d affects second allele of one or more
retinal cells, resulting in multifocal a nd u sually
bilateral tumour formation. About 15% of heri table
cases are unilateral. Some heritable cases have
trilateral retinoblastoma (i.e. having asso ciated
p inealoblastoma).

- _Heritable cases can transmit the disease_ by autosomal
dominant way to 50% of offsprings. However, due to
variable penetrance only 40% are affected.
2 . _No_ _11-lr_ _eritab/e_ _or somatic cases._ About 60% of

retinoblasto ma cases occur sporadically by both
hit:s (mutations) occurring in the same retinal cell
in the e mbryo after fertilization. The e mutations
genera lly result in unifocal and unilateral rumou rs
which cannot be passed on to the offsprings. Such
patients are not predisposed for nonocular tumours.

**Pathology**
_Or-igi n._ Re tin o blas tom a ar ises a s ma ligna n t
proliferation of the immature retinal neural cells
which are small round cells with large nuclei, i.e. it is a

rumour of a group called _small round blue cell tumour._
_**Histopatlwlogy.**_ The tumour chiefly co nsists of small
ro1L1nd cells with large nuclei, resembling the cells of
the nuclear layer of retina. These cells may present as a
highly undifferentiated or well-di£ferent.iated tumour.

 - _Microscopic features of a well-differentiated tumour_
include Flexner-Wintersteiner rose Iles, (highly
specific of retinoblastoma), Homer-Wright rosettes,
p seudo rosettes a nd tleurenes formation (Fig. 12.34 ).

 - _Other histologic features_ are presence of areas of
necrosis and calcification.

Homer-Wright
rosette

..------- Undifferentiated
tumour cells
Flexner-wintersteiner
rosette

**Clinical features**
Common presenting features of retinoblastoma are
listed in Table 12.4. These can be described as below:

I. _**Manifestations of lntraocu/ar stage of**_
_**retinob/astoma**_
Presentation of this stage can be divided into _tv.ro_
subgroups: quiescent presentations and painful red
eye presentations.

**a. Quiescent presentations** incl ude:

l. _Leucocoria or yellowish-white pupillary reflex_
(also called as _arnaurotic cat's eye appearance)_ is the
commonest presenting feature (60%) (Fig. 12.35).
2. _Sq u int,_ us ua ll y conve rge nt, is th e second
commonest mode of presenta tion.
3. _Nystagmus_ is a rare fearure, noticed in bilateral
cases.
4. _Defective vision._ Very rarely, when th e rumour
arises late (3-5 years of age), the child may complain
of defective vision.

**Table 12.4** _Presenting features of retinoblastoma_

_**Presenting features**_

Leucocoria 60%

Strabismus 20%

Painful red eye 07%

Poor vision 05%

Asymptomatic 03%

Orbital cellulitis 03%

Unilateral mydriasis 02%

Heterochromia iridis 01 %

Hyphema 01 %

_retinoblastoma_

**Ophthalmoscopic features of intraocular retinoblastoma.**
In the e arly stages, before th e app earance of
leucocoria, fundus examination after full mydriasis
performed in children presenting with strabismus,
poo r vis ion, or other quiescent features, or on
screening in predisposed children, may reveal the
growth. Ophthalmoscopic signs in three types of
retinoblastoma are as fo llows:
i. _Endophyt,ic retinoblastoma_ (Fig. 12.36A): It grows
inwards from the retina into the vi treous cavity. On
ophthalmoscopic examination, the ru mour looks
like a well-circumscribed polypoidal mass of white
or pearly pi nk colour ( Fig. 12.368). Fine blood vessels
and some ti mes a haemorrhage may be present on its
surface. ln 1the presence of calcification, it gives the
typical _'cottage cheese'_ _appearance._ There may be
multi ple growths projecting into the vitreous, which
may seed into the gel.
ii. _Exophytic retinoblastoma_ (Fig. 12.36D). lt grows
outwards and separates the retina from the choroid. On
fundus examination il gives appearance of exudative
retinal detachment (Fig. 12.36E, _also See_ page 312).
iii. _**Mixed endophy**_ _**lic a**_ _**nd exophy**_ _**tic**_ growth pattern
having feat ures of both is also repo rted in so me
cases.
**iv.** _**Diffuse in.filtrating**_ _**tumours**_ show just a placo id
thickness of retina and not a mass. Such cases are
usually diagnosed late.

b. Painful red eye presentations. When retinoblastoma
is left un treated during the quiescent stage, some
patients m ay prese nt with severe pain, red ness,
and watering. These symptoms occur either due 10
acute secondary glaucoma or apparent intraocular
inflammation or orbital cellulitis.

- _Acute secondary glaucoma_ may occur either due to
ru mour pushing the lens-iris diap hragm forward
or tu mour cells clogging the trabecular meshwork

**316** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE ANO OCULAR ADNEXA

_endophytic and exophytic retinoblastoma, respectively_

or neovascular glaucoma. In this stage, eyeball is
enlarged _(buphthalmos)_ with apparent proptosis,
conjunctiva is congested, cornea become hazy,
intraocular pressure is raised.
_•Apparent intraocular inflammation._ Occasionally,
picture simulating severe, acute uveitis usually
associated with pseudohypopyon and/ or hyphaema
may be the presenting mode _(relinoblastoma_
_masquerading as iridocyclitis)._
_•Orbital inflammation,_ _mimicking preseptal_
_or_ _Orbital_ _cellulitis_ like p rese ntat ion may

occur with n ecrotic tumours. It does not imply
extraocular extension and the exact mechanism
is not known.

**II.** _**Manifestations of extraocular extension of**_
_**retinoblastoma**_
Diue to progressive enlargement of rumour, the globe
bursts through the sci era, usually near the limbus or
near the optic disc. It is followed by rapid fu ngation
and involvement of extraocular tissues resulting in
marked proptosis (Fig. 12.37).

**Ill.** _**Manifestations of distant metastasis of**_
_**retinoblastoma**_
It is characterised by the involvement of distant
structures as follows:
1. _Lymphatic spread_ first occurs in the preauricular

and neighbouring lymph nodes.
2. _Direct extension_ by continuity to the optic nerve

and brain is common.

**Classifications of retinoblastoma**

i. **Reese-Ellsworthclassification,** which was of
prognostic significance for the control oflocal disease
has become irrelevant with the availability of newer
chemotherapeutic agents and modes of therapy, and
so not described here.
iL International classification ofretinoblastoma (JCRB),
which is presently being followed worldwide to

decide the treatment modality, is given below:
_Group A (very low_ _**risk):**_ includes a ll _small tumours_
<3 mm in greatest dimension, confined to retina,
located >3 mm from fovea and > 1.5 mm from the
optic _disc._
_Group B (low risk):_ includes large tumours >3 mm
in dimension, and _any size tumours_ located <3 mm
from fovea, and <1.5 mm from the opti c disc margin.
_Group_ C _(moderate risk):_ includes retinoblasroma
with _focal seeds_ characterized by subretinal and or
vitreous seeds :,;3 mm from the retinoblastoma.
_Group D (high risk):_ includes _retinoblastoma with_
_diffuse seeds_ characterized by subretinal and or
vitreous >3 mm seeds from the retinoblastoma.
_Group E (very high risk):_ includes extensive relinoblastoma characterized by any of the following:
rumour touching the lens, neovascular glaucoma,
tumour anterior to anterior vitreous face involving
ciliary body and anterior segment, diffuse infiltrating

tumour, opaque media with haemo rrhage, tumour
necrosis with asep tic orbital cellulitis, invasion of
postlaminar optic nerve, choroid, sclera, orbit, and
anterior clhamber, or phthisis bulbi.

**Differenti;al diagnosis**

J. _Differe_ _ntial diagnosis of leucocoria._ Various
condilions other than retinoblastoma, which present
as leucowria are collectively called as _'pseudoglioma.'_
A few common conditions are congenital cataract,
inflamma1to11r deposits in vitreous following a plastic
cyclitis or choroiditis, coloboma of the choroid, the
retrolental fibroplasia (retinopathy of prematurity),
persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous, toxocara
endophthalmitis and exudative retinopathy of Coats.
2. _Endophytic reti11oblnstoma_ discovered on fund us
examination should be differentiated from _retinal_
_tumours_ in tuberous sclerosis and neurofibromatosis,
astrocytorna and a patch of exudative choroiditis.
3. _Exophyt,ic reli11oblastoma_ should be differen tiated
from other causes of exudativc retinal detachment
in children such as: Coats' disease _(See_ pages 294
and 312).

**Diagnosis**

1. _Exami,wtion under ana_ _esthesia:_ It sho uld be
performed in all clinically suspected cases. It should
include ftmdus examination of both eyes after full
mydriasis with atropine (direct as well as indirect
opht halnrnscopy), measure me nt of intraocular
pressure and corneal diameter.

2. _Plain X-mys of orbit_ may show calcification which
occurs in 75% cases of relinoblastoma.
3. _Lactic dehydrogenase (L Dfl)_ level is raised in
aqueous humour.
4. _Ultrasonography and CT/ MRI scanning_ are very
useful in the diagnosis. CT/ MRJ also demonstrate
extension to optic nerve, orbit and CNS, if any
(Figs 12.315( and F). However, CT sho uld be used
sparin gly beca use of potential risk of radiation
sarcomas.

**Treatment·**
_**A. Conservative tumour destructive therapy to**_
_**salvage eyeball**_
It is ind icated when tumour is diagnosed in (stages
A to D of lCRB). Present recommendations for the
treatmem are _**mu.ltimodal therapy**_ comprising
primary systemic chemotherapy for chemoreduction
fo llowed by focal therapy for consolidation.
**Chemotherapy.** Dose in mg/ kg body weigh t for
chemored uction of retinoblastoma are as below:
_•Standard dose CVE regimen,_ recommended for
group A, B, and, C palients, consists of 3-weekly, 6
cycles of carboplatin ( 18.6 mg) on day 1, vincristinc

**318** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

(0.05 mg) on day 1, and etop oside (5 mg) on day 1
and 2.

- _High dose CVE regimen,_ recommended for group
D patients, consists of 3-weekly, 6-12 cycles of
carboplatin (28 mg) on day 1, vincristine (0.25 mg)
on day 1 and etoposide (12 mg) on day 1 and 2.
Focal therapy. Depending upon the location and size
of the tumour, focal therapy can be chosen from the
following modalities:
Cryotherapy is indicated for a small tumour located
anterior to equator.
Laser photocoagulation is used for a small rumour
located posterior to equator.
Thcrmotherapy with diode laser is used for a small

tumour loca ted posterior to eq uator away from
macula.
Plaque radiotherapy is very effective against localised
vitreous disease and for the elevated tumou rs when

laser is ineffective.
External beam radfotherapy (EBR), once the mainstay
of treatment, is now reserved for diffuse disease in
the only remaining eye.
**Note.** If the above modalities are not available, the
eyeball should be enucleated without hesitation.

_**8. Enucleation**_
It is the treannent of choice for group E nunors and
when:

- Tumour invo lves more than half of the retina.

- Optic nerve is involved.

- Glaucoma is present and anterior chamber is
involved.

Eyeball should be enucleated along with maximum
length of the optic nerve taking special care not to
perforate the eyeball. _Jf_ _optic nerve shows invasion,_
postoperative treatment should include:

 - _External beam radiotherapy(S,000_ rads) should be
applied to the orbital apex.

 - _Chemotherapy,_ consistingofcarboplatin, vincristine,
and etoposide (CVE) which may be combin ed V\iith
cyclosporin should be supplemented.

**C.** _**Palliative therapy**_
It is given in following cases where prognosis for life
is dismal in spite of aggressive treatment:

- Retinoblastoma with orbital extension,

- Retinoblastoma with intracranial extension, and

- Retinoblastoma with distant metastasis.
_**Palliative therapy**_ should include combination of:

 - _Chemoreduction_ (CVE regimen),

 - _Surgical debulkingof_ the orbit or orbital exenteration,
and

 - _External beam radiotherapy_ (EBHT).

**Note.** _**Exe**_ _**ntera tion of the**_ _**orbit**_ (a mu til ati ng
surgery commonly performed in the past) is now not
preferred by many surgeons.

**Prognosis**
_If uritreated_ the prognosis is almost _always_ bad and
the patient invariably d ies. Rarely _spontaneous_
_regression_ with res ulta nt cure and s hrinkage of
the eyeba ll may occur due to necrosis fo llowed by
calcification; suggesting role of some _immunological_
_phenomenon_ (tumour im munity).
_**1f the eyeball**_ _is_ _**enucleated**_ befo re the occurrence of
extraocular extension, prognosis is fair (survival rate
70-85%).

_**Poor prognostic factors**_ are: Optic nerve involvement,
undifferentiated t umour cells and massive choroidal
invasion.

## Retinal Astrocytoma

Astrocytoma, also known as astrocytic hamartoma,
can rarely involve the retina or optic n erve head.
It may occur as an isolated tumor of retina, or in
association with:

- Tuberous sclerosis (most frequent) _(See_ page 320)

- Neurofibromatosis rype I

- Retinitis pigrnentosa
_**Origin.**_ It is an acqu ired benign tum or that arises
from the astrocytes within the retinal nerve fibre
layer.

_**Clinical features.**_ Most astrocytomas are asymptomatic
and are detected on a routine fundus examination
or on screening of tuberous sclerosis. Syn dromic
asu·ocytomas are usually mu.ltifocal and bilateral while
non-syndromic asu·ocytoma is unilateral and unifocal.
_**Ophtl1almoscopic features**_ Astrocy tomas look as
yellowish white plaque, nodule or mulberry like

lesion. It is often serniu•ansparent in periphery and
calcified cenn·ally. Sometimes, it may be large and
may even have cystic components.
_Treat11umt_ is generally not required.

## Retinal Vascular Tumours

## Capillary Haemangioma

Capillary haema ngioma of retina is rare sight
threatening benign acqu ired tumor. It may present
as a solita ry or multiple !es.ions. Almost 50% of
soli tary tumors a nd almost a ll mu lti ple tumou rs
are a part ofVon-1 lippel Li ndau (VH L) disease _(See_
page 319)

**Clinical features**
Retinal capillary haemangioma may be d etected
on routine fund us examination or because of visual
symptoms d ue to macular exudates.

**319**

Ophthalmoscopic features
_Features of tumors_

 - _Early tumors appear as small red oval or round_
_lesion_ located between the a11eriole.

 - _A well-established lumor_ presents as round orange
red mass located tempora lly in the superior or
inferior quadranL or near the temporal margin of
the optic disc (juxtapapillary).
_Features of complications._ Leakage with exudate
formation and /or retinal haemorrhage are quite
common and cause decreased vision due to macular
exudates.
_Vitreous_ haemorrhages, fibrotic bands and tractional
retinal detachment may also be seen.

**Treatment**
_Juxta-papillary_ _tumors_ are best treated with

photodynamic therapy (PDT).
_Posteriorly Located tumors,_ away from disc and fovea
are best treated with laser photocoagulation.
_Peripheral tumors_ located anterior to the equator are
best treated with cryotherapy.
_Large tumors_ need to be treated with brachytherapy.
_Anterior complications,_ like non-absorbing vitreous
haemorrhage, tractiona1 bands and Lractional RD,
require vitreoretinal surgery.

## Cavernous Haemangioma Of Retina

Cavernous haemangioma of retina and optic disc
head is a rare unilateral congenital hamartoma.
_Clillical f eatures._ Cavern ous h aemang iom a
may present as loss of vision d ue to vitreous
haemorrhage or mostly is discovered on routine
fund us examination. It may involve any part of retina,
occasionally optic nerve.
_Ophthalmoscopic features._ Retinal cavernous
haemangioma is a c luster of ane urysms with
associa ted greyish tiss ue, rese mbling a bunch
of grapes in appearance. Fluid levels in the form
menisci are also seen in the lesion.
_Co_ _mplication_ _s_ in th e form of h ae m orrhages
and epiretinal membrane (ERM) fo rm ation can
occur.

## Primary Vitreoretinal Lymphoma

Primary vitreoretinal lymphoma (PVRL) is a
distinct subtype of primary central nervous system
lymphoma (PCNSL). The ma lignant lymphoid
cells in this disorder are usually of _B-cell lineage._
Primary PVRL, is a vari ant of _e..--ctended non-1 lodgkin_
_lymphoma._
Clinical features. PVRL presents as masquerade
syndrome as it is misdiagnosed as uveitis, because
of following clinical feamres.

**Symptoms** include unilateral blurred vision,
photophobia. ln many cases, symptoms soon become
bilateral.
Signs noted include:

 - _Mild anterior uveitis_ with K.Ps, anterior chamber
cells and fiarc.

 - _Vitritis,_ mild to severe, which obscures fundus view.

 - _ubretinal infiltrates_ are large and multifocal.

 - _Retinal vasculature_ may be seen.

 - _Exudalive retinal detachment_ may be seen in some
cases.

 - _Vascular occlusions_ are also known.

 - _Absence of cystoid macular oedema,_ is a conspicuous
feature which is a consistent feature of true uveitis.

 - _CNS_ involvement also occurs ultimately in most
of the cases.
**Diagnosis** in a suspected case on OCT, FA, USG and
MRI is confirmed by _cytology of vitreous_ samples or
subretinal nodule.
Treatment. Systemic and local chemotherapy and
radiotherapy are the mainstay. Eye and brain need
to be treated simultaneously.

## Retinal Pigment Epithelium

## Tumours

**RPE tumours include:**

- Congenital hypertrophy ofR PE

- Congenital simple hamartoma ofRPE

- Combined hamartoma ofretina and RPE

- Adenoma and adenocarcinoma ofRPE

- Hyperplasia and migration of RPE simulating uveal
melanoma.

## Phacomatoses

Phacomatoses or neurocutaneous syndromes refer
to a group of familial conditions (having autosomal
dominant transmission) which are characterised by
development of neoplasms in eye, skin and central
nervous system.

**Note.** Phacomatosis is covered here, since in each
type there is involvement of retina. Phacomatoses
includes the fo llowing conditions:

**1. ANGIOMATOSIS RETINAE (VON HIPPEL**

## Lindau Syndrome)

This is a rare condition affecting males more often

than females, in the third and fourth decade of life.
_•Angiomatosis involues_ retina, brain, spinal cord,
kidneys and adrenals.

- _Clinical course of angiomatosis rerinae_ comprises
vascular dilatation, tortuosity and formation of
aneurysms which vary from small and miliary to
balloon-like angiomas, followed by appearance of

**320** SECTION Ill DISEAS ES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

haemorrhages and exudates, resembli ng eventually
the exudative relinopathyof Coats. Massive exudation
is frequently complicated by retinal detachment
which may be prevented by an early destruction of
angiomas with cryopexy or photocoagulation.

**2. TUBEROUS SCLEROSIS (BOURNEVILLE**

## Disease)

It is characterised by a classic diagnostic triad of
adenoma sebaceum, mental retardation and epilepsy
associated with hamartom as of the brain, retina
and viscera. The name tuberous sclerosis is derived
from the potato-like appea ran ce of the tumours
in the cerebrum and other organs. Two types of
_hamartomas found in_ I _he retina are-._ (I) relatively flat
and soft appearing white or grey lesions usually seen
in the po terior pole; and (2) large nodular tumours
havi ng predilection for the region of the optic disc.

**3. NEUROFIBROMATOSIS (VONRECKLINGHAUSEN'S DISEASE)**
It is characterised by multiple tumours in the ski n,

nervous system and other organs.

- _Cutaneous manifestations_ are very characteristic
and vary from cafe-au-lait spots to neurofibromata.

- _Ocular manifestations_ incl ude neurofibromas of the
lids and orbit, glioma of oplic nerve and congenital
glaucoma.

**4. ENCEPHALOFACIAL ANGIOMATOSIS(STURGE-WEBER SYNDROME)**

It is characterised by angiomatosis in the fo rm
of port-wine stain (naev us llammeus), involving

**m**

one side of the face which may be associated with
choroidal haemangioma, leptomeningeal angioma
and congenital glaucoma on the affected side.

## Enucleation

£nucleation refers to excision of the eyeball. It can
be performed under local anaesthesia in adults and
u nder general anaesthesia in ch ildren.

**Indications**
1. _Absolute indications_ a re retinoblastoma and

malignant melanoma.
2. _Relative indications_ are painful blind eye, non
responsive to conservative measure mutilating
ocular injuries, anterior staphyloma and phthisis
bulbi.
3. _/11d ication fo_ _r_ _eye do_ _nation_ from cad aver is

presently the most co mmon indicatio n for
enucleation.

**Surgical technique**

I . _Separation of co11jtt11ctiva_ _and Tenon's capsule_
(Fig. 12.38A): Conjunctiva is incised all around 1.he
limbuswith the help ofspri ng scissors. Un derm ining
of the conjunctiva and Tenon's ca psule is done
combinedly, a ll arou nd up to the equator, using
blu nt-tip ped c urved scissors. This m anoeuvre
exposes the extraocular muscles.
2. _Separation ojextraocular muscles_ (Fig. 12.388): The
rectus muscles are pulled out one by one with the
help of a muscle hook and a 3- 0 silk sutme is passed

near the insertion of each muscle. The muscle is then
cut with th e help ofrenotomy scissors leaving behind

_extraocular muscles; C, Cutting of optic nerve and removal of eyeball; D, Insertion of an orbital implant; and E. Closure_
_of the conjunctiva_

a sma ll stump carrying the suture. The inferior and
superior oblique muscles are hooked out and cut
near the globe.
3. _Cutting of optic nerve_ (Fig. 12.38C): The eyebaJl is
prolapsed out by stretching and pushing down the
eye speculum. The eyeball is pulled out with the
help of sutures passed through the muscle stumps.
The cnucleation scissors is then introduced along

the medial wall up to the posterior aspect of the
eyeball. Optic nerve is felt and then cut with the
scissors while maintaining a constant pull on the
eyeball.

4. _Removal of eyeball._ The eyeball is pulled out of the
orbit by incising the remaining tissue adherent to it
and _haemostasis_ is achieved by packing the orbital
cavity with a wet pack and pressing it back.
5. _T11serting an orbital implant_ (Fig. 12.380 ): Preferably
an orbital implant (made up of PMMA Medpor or
hydroxyapatite) of appropriate size should be inserted
into the orbit and sutured with the rectus muscles.
6. _Closure of conj_ _unctiva and Tenon_ _'s capsule_ is don e
separately. Tenon's capsule is sutured horizontally
with 6-0 vicryl or chromic catgut. Conjun ctiva is
sutured vertically so that conjunctiva! fornices are
retained deep with 6-0 silk sutures (Fig. 12.38£)
which are removed after 8-10 days. After completion
of surgery, antibiotic ointment is applied, lids are
closed and dressing is done 'Ari th firm pressure using
sterile eye pads and a bandage.

_artificial eye; B,_ _With artificial eye_

**Fitting of artificial prosthetic eye**
Conformer may be used postoperatively so that
the conjunctiva! fornices are reta in ed deep. A
proper sized prosthetic eye can be inserted for good
cosmetic appearance (Fig. 12.39) after 6 weeks when
healing of the enucleated socket is complete.

## Anatomy And Physiology

## Anatomy Of Visual Pathway

**Components of visual pathway**
The visual pathway starting from retina consists

of optic nerves, optic chiasma, optic tracts, lateral
geniculate bodies, optic radiations and the visual
cortex (Fig. L3. 1 ).

**Optic nerve**
Each optic nerve (second cranial nerve) starts fro m
the optic disc and extends up to optic chiasma, where
the two nerves meet. It is the backward continuation
of the nerve fibre layer of the retina, which consists of
Lhe axons originating fro m the ganglion cells ( second
order neuron). It also contains the afferent fibres of
the pupillary light reflex.

**Morphologically and embryologically,** the optic nerve
is comparable to a sensory uact:

 - _Not covered by neu.rilemma_ un li ke peripheral
nerves. So, it does not regenerate when cut.

- _Fibres of optic nerve,_ numbering about a million,
_are very fine_ (2-10 µmin diam eter as compared to
20 µm of sensory nerves).

 - _Covered by meningeal sheaths_ like other parts of
C S.

- Day blindness Hemeralopia

- Colour blindness

- Amaurosis

- Amblyopia

- Cortical blindness

- Malingering

- Hysterical blindness

- Disorders of higher visual functions
OCULAR MANIFESTATIONS OF DISEASES OF CENTRAL

## Nervous System

- lntracran,al infections

- lntracranial aneurysms

- lntracran1al haemorrhages

- mracranial space-occupy,ng lesions (ICSOLs)

- Demyelinating diseases

- Ocular signs in head injury

Parts of optic nerve. The opt ic nerve is about
47- 50 mm in length, and can be d ivided into 4
parts: intraocular (I mm ), in traorbital (30 mm),
intracanalicular (6-9 mm) and in tracraniaJ (10 mm).

I . _**f11traocu/ar part**_ starts fro m lhe optic disc _(See_ page
27l), pierces Lhe choroid and sclera (converti ng it
inlo a sieve-like structure the lamina cribrosa). At
the back of eyeball, it becomes continuous with the
intraorbital part.
2. _flltraorbital part_ extends from back of the eyeball
to the optic foramina. This part is slightly sinuous to
give play for the eye movements. Posteriorly, near
the optic foramina, it is closely su rrounded by the
annulus of Zinn an d the origin of th e four rectus
m uscles. Some fibres of superior rectus muscle
are adherent to its sheath here, and accounts for
the painful ocular movements seen in retrobulbar
neuritis. Anteriorly, the nerve is separated from the
ocular muscles by the orbital fat.
**3_** _**llltra ca 11alicular part**_ is closely related to the
ophthalmic artery which Hes inferolateral to it and
crosses obliquely over it, as it enters the orbit, to lie
on its medial side. Sphcnoid and posterior ethm oidal
sinuses lie medial to it and are separated by a thin
bony lamina. This relation accounts for retrobulbar

neuritis fo llowing infection of the sinuses.

**4.** _I11tracra11ial part_ of the optic nerve lies above the
cavernous sinus and converges wid1 its fellow (over
the diaphragma sellae) to fo rm the optic chiasma.

Meningeal sheaths. Pia mater, arachnoid and dura
covering the brain are continuous over the optic
nerves. In the optic canal, the dura is firmly adherent
with the surrounding bone. The subarachnoid and
subd ural spaces around the optic n erve are also
continuous wim those of the brain.

**Optic chiasma**
It is a flattened stru ctu re measu rin g 12 mm
(horizontally) and 8 mm (ameroposteriorly). It lies
directly above me tuberculum and diaphragma sellae
and, therefore, presence of visual field defects in a
patient with pituitary tumor indicates suprasellar
extension.

Fibres origin ating from the nasal halves of the
retina decussate at the chiasma (Fig. 13.1).

**Optic tracts**
These are cylindrical bundles of nerve fibres running
outwards and backwards from the posterolateral
aspect ofme optic chiasma. _Each optic tract consisls_
_of fibres from the lemporal half of the retina of the_
_same eye and the nasal half of the opposile eye._
Posceriorly, each optic tract ends in the lateral
geniculate body (Fig. 13.1 ). The pupillary reflex fibres

pass on to pretectal nucleus in the mid brain mrough
me superior brachium. Some fibres termin ate in the
superior colliculus _(See_ Fig. 13.6).

**Lateral geniculate bodies**
These are oval structures situated at the posterior

cermination of the optic tracts. Each geniculate

Optic nerve

Optic chiasma

Optic tract

Lateral
geniculate body

Optic radiation

body consists of six layers of neurons (grey matter)
alrernatirng with white matter (formed by optic
fibres). The fibres of second-order neurons coming
via optic Lracts relay in ilicse third-order neurons.

**Optic radi;~tions**
Optic radiations (gcniculocalcarine pathway) extend
from ilie lateral geniculate bodies to the visual cortex
and consist of the axons of third-order neurons of
visual palhway (Fig. 13. l ). Inferior fibres of optic
radiations subserve upper visual fields and superior
fibres subserve infe rior visual fields.

**Visual cor1tex**
It is l.ocated on the medial aspect of the occipital
lobe, above and below the calcarine fissure. It is
subdivided into the visuosenso1y area (striate area

17) that receives the fibres uf d1e radiations, and the
surrounding visuopsychic area (peristriate area 18
and paras1triate area 19).

**Blood supply of the visual pathway**
The visual pathway is mainly s upplied by pial

network o f vessels except the orbital part of optic
nerve whkh is also supplied by an axial system
derived from the central artery of retina. The pial
plexus around different parts of the visual pamway
gets contribution from different arteries as shown

in Fig. 13.~!.
_Blood supirJly of the optic nerve h_ _ead_ needs special
mention (!Fig. 13.3):

- _Surface_ _layer_ of the optic disc is supplied by
capillaries derived from the retinal arterioles.

- _Prelamirwr region_ is mainly supplied by centripetal
branches of the peripapi llary choroid with some
concribu ti on from me vessels of lamina cribrosa.

The pathway of somatic as we ll as vis ual

## Lesions Of Visual Pathway

Salient features and important causes of lesions of

the visual pathway al di fferent levels (Fig. 13.5) are
as follows:

_(8) versus somatic sensations_ _**(A)**_

## Optic Nerve Lesions

**1. Lesions of distal part of optic nerve**
_**Salie11lfeatures**_ are:

- Ma rked loss o f vision o r com plete blin dness
on the affected side associated _(See_ 1 and A in

- Abolition of the direct light reflex on the ipsilateral
side and consensual on the contralateral side.

- ear (accommodation) reflex is present.
_**Common causes**_ of optic nerve lesions are: oplic
atrophy, traumatic avulsion of the o ptic nerve,
indirect optic neuropathy, ischemic oplic neuropathy
and acute optic neuritis.

**2. L,~sions of proximal part of optic nerve.**
_**Salie11tfeatures**_ of such lesions are:

- Ip:silateral blindness,

- Contralateral hemianopia, this is because while
decussating in lhe optic chiasma some nasal fibres
loop in the proximal part of opposite optic nerve
_(S1ee_ 2 in Fig. 13.5).

- Abolition of direct light reflex on the affected side
and consensual on the conn·alateral side, and

- Near reflex is intact.

**C:HIASMAL LESIONSCauses of chiasmal lesions**

I. _Intrinsic causes,_ i.e. which p roduce thickening

o,f chiasma itself include gliomas, and multiple
sclerosis.
2. _Extrinsic causes_ include compressive lesions such

as pituitary adenoma (most common cau e),
craniopharyngiomas, and meningioma.

3. _Other causes of chiasmal syndrome_ include meta
bolic, toxic, trawnatic, and inflammatory conditions
(lymphoid hypophysitis and sarcoidosis).

**Features of chiasmal lesions**
_**Chiasmal syndrome**_
Chiasmal syndrome refers to the set of signs and
symptoms associated with lesions of optic chiasma.
Chiasmal syndrome has been classified into Lhree
types:
I. _**Anterior cl1iasmal syndrome**_ is produced by lesions
that affect the ipsilateral optic nerve fibres and
the contralateral inferonasal fibres located in the
Willebrand knee; typically producing the so-called
_junctional scotoma,_ i.e. a combination of central

scotoma in one eye and temporal hemianopia defect
in the other eye. (Fig. 13.SB)

2. _Middle,chiasmal syndrome_ is produced by lesions

invo lvin~i the decussating fibres in the body of
chi as ma typically producing _bitemporal hemiarwpia_
(Fig. 13.SC) and bitemporal hemianopic paralysis of
pupillary reflexes.

**3.** _**Posteriorchiasmal sy11drome**_ **is** produced by lesions

affecting the caudal fibres in chiasma. _Characteristic_
_features_ rure as below:

 - _Paracentral bi temporal field defects_ occur because
the macular fibres which cross more posteriorly in
the chiasma are damaged in the posterior chiasmal
lesions.

 - _Visual acuity_ and _colour vision_ may be preserved as
temporal macular fibres arc not, damaged.

 - _Hornonymous hemianopia_ on the contralateral side
may occur when posterior chiasmal lesions involve
the optic tract (Fig. 13.SE).

**326** SECTION Ill DISEASES OF EYE AND OCULAR ADNEXA

_**Lateral chiasmal lesions**_
_Salient features_ of such lesions are:

 - _Binasal hemianopia_ (Fig. 13.50) associated with

 - _Bi nasal hemianopic_ paralysis of the pu pillary
reflexes. These usually lead tO partial descending
optic atrophy.
_Common causes_ of such lesions are:

 - Distension of third ventricle causing pressure on
each side of the chiasma and

 - Ad1eroma of the carotids or posterior communicating arteries.

## Retrochiasmal Lesions

_Retrochiasmal lesions_ include lesions of optic tract,
lateral gen iculate nucleus, optic radiations and
occipital lobe.
_Contralateml homonymous hemiauopia_ of different
forms such as incomplete ( congruous or uncongruous)
or complete, depending upon the site of lesion is the
predominant field defect of all retrochiasmal lesions.

**Lesions of optic tract**
_Causes_ of optic tract lesions include:

 - _Intrinsic lesions_ include demyelinaring di seases
and infarclion. Such lesions produce optic tract
syndrome type IT.

 - _Extrin sic,_ i.e . _compressive_ _lesions_ in cl ude
pituitary adenom as (most comm on ca u se),
cra niopharyngioma (co m mon), tumours of
optic thalamus, and large an eurysm of superi or
cerebellar or posterior cerebral arteries.

 - _Other causes_ include syphilitic meningitis, gumma
and tubercular meni ngitis.
_**Characteristic features**_ include:

 - _Inco_ _ngruous_ _h_ _omonymous_ _hemian_ _opia,_
_i.e._ asymmetrical fi eld defect involving either
right halves of visual field of both eyes (right
homonymous h emianopia (Fig. 13.SE) in left optic
tract lesions) or left halves of visual fields of both
eyes (left h omonymous hemianopia du e to lesions
of right optic tract). This is explained anatomically
by the fact that each optic tract contains fibres
from the ipsilateral temporal half of retina a nd
contra lateral nasal half of retina.

- _Contralateral hemianopic pupillary responses,_ i.e.
Wernicke's reaction.

- _Optic disc changes._ Oplic tract lesions produce
descending typ e of partial optic atrophy
characterized by temporal pallor on the side of
lesion a nd bow-Lie atrophy on the contralateral
side.

- _Visual acuity_ is usually intact in the intrinsic lesions
of optic tract.

 - _Ipsilatera/ third nerve_ _palsy_ and _ipsilateral_
_hemiplegia_ may be associated with optic tract
lesions in some cases.

**Lesions of lateral geniculate nucleus**
Characteristic features of lateral geniculatc nucleus
(LG ) lesions are as below:

_• Jlomonymous hemianopia_ produced is usually
incongruous (Fig. 13.SE).

 - _Pupillary reflexes_ are normal, as fibres for pupillary
re flexes from the optic tract are diverted to pretectal
nucleus and do not reach the LGN.

 - _Opticdiscpa/lor_ _mayoccurdue_ to partial descending
a trophy.

**Lesions of optic radiationsCommon lesions** of optic radiations include:

 - Vascular occlusions,

- Tumours (primary or secondary),

- Trauma, and

- Temporal lobect0my for seizures.

**Characteristic features** include:

**J.** _Visual field defects_ in the lesions of opric radiations
va ry depending upon the site oflesion as below:

 - _Superior quadrantic hemianopia_ (pie in the sky)
(Fig. l 3.5E) is produced when _inferior fibres_ are
involved in _temporal lobe lesions._ Anatomically, it
is explained by the fact that inferior fi bres of optic
radiarions contain fibres from ipsilateral lower
temporal retina and con tralateral lowe r n asal
reti na. Inferior most fibres of optic radiaLion form
the so ca lled _Meyer's loop._

- _Inferior quadmntic hemianopia_ (pie on the floor)
(Fig. 13.5G) is produced when _superiorflbres_ are
involved in _parietal lobe lesions._ This is explained
anatomically by the fact th at superior fibres of
optic radiations contain fi bres from ipsilateral
upper temporal retina and contralateral upp er
nasal retina.

- _Complete homonymous hemianopia,_ (Fig. 13.5H)
is produced when total fibres of optic radiations,
are involved sometimes with sparing of macula
(as macular fibres lie in the central part of optic
radiations).
**2.** _**Pupillary reflexes are spared**_ in lesions of optic
radiations.
**3.** _**Optic disc atrophy does not occur**_ in lesions of optic
radiations, as the second order neurons synapse in
the lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN).

**Lesions of visual cortex**
Characteristic features of visual cortex lesions can
be described as visua l field de fects an d other
manifestati ons.

I. _Visual.field defects_ in _occipital lobe lesions_ include:

- _Congruous homonymous hemianopia_ (Fig. 13.5T)
( usually sparing the ma cul a) is a fea ture of occlusion
of posterior cerebral artery supplying the anterior
part of visual cortex.

- _Congruous homonymous macular defects_ (Fig.
13.5)) occur in lesions of the tip of occipital cortex
following head injury or gunshot injuries.

- _Bilateral homonymous hemianopia with macula_
_sparing_ producing a picture of ring scotoma is seen
in bilateral occipital lobe lesions.

- _Bilateral homonymuus macular defects,_ presenting
like bilateral central scotoma (Fig. 13.5K) occur in
bilateral lesions of occipital cortex.

**ote.** Pupillary light reflexes are normal and optic
atrophy does not occur foll owing visual co rtex
lesions.
II. _Other manifestations of occipital lobe lesions_ include:

- _Cortical blindness_ can occur in bilateral occipital
lobe lesions.

- _Dyschromatopsia,_ i.e. acquired colour blindness.

- _Visual hallucinations_ occurring in occipital lobe
lesions arc unfor med, while in temp oral lobe
lesions the visual hallucinations are formed.

- _Palinopsia,_ i.e. pe rsistent or recu rren t perception
of a visual image, may occur.

- _Visual anaesthesia,_ i.e. transpositio ning of visual
stimuli from one hemifield to another.

- _Polyopsia,_ i.e. multiple images of a single object,
seen in occipital lobe lesions, does not disappear
on closing of eye.

## Pupillary Reflexes And Their

## Abnormalities

## Pupillary Reflexes

**Light reflex**
When light is shone in one eye, both the pupils
constrict. Constriction of the pupil to which light is
shone is called _direct Light reflex_ and that of the other
pupil is called _consensual (indirect) light reflex._ Light
reflex is initiated by rods and cones.

Pathway of light reflex include (Fig. 13.6):
l. _Afferent.fibres_ extend from rcrina to the prctectal
nucleus in the mid brain. These travel along the opri c
nerve to the optic chiasma where fibres from the
nasal retina decussate and travel along the opposite
optic tract lo terminate in the contra lateral pretectaJ
nucleus. While the fibres from the temporal retina
remain uncrossed and travel along the optic tract of
the sam e side to terminate in the ipsilateral pretectal
nucleus.

C,hary ganglion

Third nerve

Opt~ tract

Central grey matter---iu,

Aqueduct

Lateral geniculate

body

comm,ssure

**F'ig. 13.6** _Pathway of the light reflex_

2. _Jntemw,cialfibres_ connect each pretectal nucleus
with Edinger-Westphal nuclei of both sides. This
connection fo rms the basis of consensual Light refl ex.
_3. Efferent /1Jathway_ consists of the parasympathetic

fibres w hi ch arise fro m the Edinge r-Westphal
nucleus in the midbrain and travel along the third
(oculomotior) cranial nerve. The preganglionic fibres
enter the imferior division of Lhe third nerve and via
the nerve to the inferior oblique reach the ciliary
ganglion to relay. Post-ganglionic fibres travel along
the short c:iliary nerves to innervate the sphincter
pupillae.

**Near refle>t**
Near reflex occurs on looking at a near object. It
consists of two components: (a) convergence reflex,
i.e. constrilction of p upil on convergence; and (b)
accommodation reflex, i.e. constriction of pupil
associated wi th accommodation.

_Pathway of co1111ergence reflex_ (Fig. 13.7). lls afferent
pathway is still not elucidared. It is assumed that the
afferents from the medial recti travel centrally via
the third nerve to the mesencephalic nucleus of the
fifth nerve,. to a presumptive convergence cenu·c in
the tectal cir pretectal region. From this, the impulse
is relayed to the Edinger-Westphal nucleus and the
subsequent efferent pathway of near reflex is along
the 3rd ner ve. The efferent fibres relay in the accessory
ganglion b,efore reaching the sphincter pupillae.
_Pathwayofaccommodationrejlex(Fig.13.7)._ The afferent
impulses extend from the retina to the parastriate
cortex via the optic nerve, chiasma, optic tract, lateral
geniculate body, optic radiations, and striate cortex.
From the p,arastriare cortex, d1e impulses are relayed
to d1e Edinger-Westphal nucleus of both sides via the
occipitomesencephalic tract and the pontine centre.
From the Edinger-Westphal nucleus the efferent
impulses travel along the 3rd nerve an d reach the
sphincter pupillae and ciliary muscle after relaying in

the accessory and ciliary ganglions.
