## Related Questions

and retinal detachment surgery in adults can be
performed under local anaesthesia.
_Goals._ The main goals of regional anaesthesia for
successful ocular surgery are:

- Globe and conjunctiva] anaesthesia,

- Orbicularis and ocular akinesia, and

- Low intraocular and intraorbital pressure.
_These goals can be achieved by_ a local anaesthes.ia
comprising either surface anaesthesia, facial block
and retrobulbar block or a combination of surface
anaesthesia and peribulbar block.

**Surface (Topical) anaesthesia**
Surface anaesthesia is achieved by topical
instillations of2 to 4% xylocaine or 1 % amethocaine
or proparacaine 0.5% (most preferred for topical
anesthesia). Usually, a drop of anaesthetic solution
instilled 4 times after every 4 minutes, is sufficient
to produce conjunctiva! and corneal anaesthesia,
which lasts for about half an hour. Cataract surgery
especially by phacoemuJsification can be successfully
performed under topical anaesthesia only.

**Facial block**
For intraocular surgery, it is necessary to block the
facial nerve which supplies the orbicularis oculi
muscle, so that patient cannot squeeze the eye!idls.
OrbicuJaris akinesia can be achieved by blocking the
facial nerve at its terminal branches (van Lint block),
superior branches (Atkinson block) or proximal
trunk (O' Brien or Nadbath block).

1. _Bfocki11g the peripheral branches of facial nerve (vmi_
_Lint 's block) :_ This technique blocks th e terminal

branches of the facial nerve, producing localized
akinesia of the orbicularis oculi mus cle withour
associated facial paralysis.

In this technique, 2.5 rnL of anaesthetic solurion
is injected in deeper tissues just above the eyebrow
and just below the inferior orbital margin, through a
point about 2 cm behind the lateral orbital margin,
level with outer can thus (Fig. 26.1 ).
2. _Facial nerve trunk block:_ At _the neck of mandible_
_(O'Brien's block)._ In it, facial nerve is blocked near
the condyloid process. The condyle is located 1 cm
anterior to the tragus. It is easily palpated if the
patient is asked to open and close the mouth with the
operator's index finger located across the neck of the
mandible. Al this point, the needle is inserted until
contact is made with the periosteum and then 4 to
6 ml of local anaesthetic is injected while the needle
is withdrawn (Fig. 26.2).

_and technique of O'Brien_ ·s _block_

This technique is associated with pain ac the
injection site and unwanted facial paralysis.
3. _**Nadba,th**_ _**block:**_ In this technique, th e facial
nerve is blocked as it leaves the skull through the
stylomastoid foramen. This block is _also_ painful.
**4.** _Atkinson's block:_ In it, superior branches of the

facial nerve are blocked by injecting anaesthetic
solution at the inferior margin of the zygomatic bone.

**Retrobulbar block**
Retrob ulb ar b lock, _now_ _sparingly_ _used,_ was
introdu ced by He rm an Knapp in 1884. It is
adm inistered by injecting 2 mL of anaesthetic
solution (2% xylocaine with added hyaluronidase
5 IU / mL and with or without adrenaline one in
one lac) into the muscle cone behind the eyeball
(Fig. 26.3A). It is usual to give the injection Uuough
the inferior fornix or the skin of outer part of lower lid
with the eye in primary gaze (Fig. 26.4A). The needle
is first directed straight backwards then slightly
upwards and inwards towards the apex of the orbit,
up to a de·pth of2.5 to 3 cm.

Retrobu lbar block anaesthetizes th e ciliary
nerves, ciliary ganglion and third and sixth cranial
nerves th us producing globe akinesia, anaesthesia
and analgesia. The superior oblique muscle is not

usually paralyzed as the fourth cranial nerve is
outside the muscle cone.
Complicatio11s encow1tered with itincluderetrobulbar
haemorrhage, globe perforation, optic nerve injury,
and extraocular muscle palsies.

**Peribulbar block**
This technique described in 1986 by Davis and
Mandel has almost replaced the time-tested
combi nation of re trob ul bar a nd facial blocks,
because of its fewer complications and by obviating
the need for a separate facial block.

_the_ muscle! _cone; B,_ _For peribulbar block m the peripheral_
_orbital space_

**614** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

_Technique,_ primarily involves Lhe injection of
6 to 7 mL of local a na es th etic so lution in the
peripheral space of the orbit (Fig. 26.3B), from where

iL di ff uses into the muscle cone and lids; leading
to globe and orbicularis akinesia and anaesthesia.
Classically, the peribulbar block is administered
by two injections; first through the upper lid (at the
junction of medial one-third and lateral two-thirds)
and second through the lower lid (at the junction of
lateral one-third and medial two-thirds) (Fig. 26.4B).
After injection djgital orbital compression or message
is appHed for 10 to 15 minutes.
_**Anaesthetic solution**_ used for peribulbar anaesthesia
consists ofa mi.xtme of2% lignocaine, and 0.5 to 0.75%
bupivacaine (in a ratio of 2: l) with hyaluronidase 5
IU/ mL and adrenaline one in one lac.

## General Anaesthesia

_**J11dications**_ include infants and children, anxious,
non-cooperative and mentally retarded ad ults,
perfo rating ocular injuries, major operations like
exenteraLion and the patients willing fo r operation
under general anaesthesia.
_fmporta11t considerations._ During general anaesthesia
for ocular surgery include:

    - Use of relaxants, e ndotracheal intubation and
controlled respiration is preferred.

    - It must be ensured that patient does not develop
carbon dioxide retention. When Lhis occurs,
choroid swells to many times its normal value
and ocular contents prolapse as soon as the eye
is opened.

_A, Retrobulbar block; 8, For peribulbar block_

- Anaesthetic agent like ketamine should be avoided
fo1r intraocular surgeries, as it raises intraocular
pressure

- ln perforating injuries and other ocular emergency
cases, use of suxamethonium should always be
pneferred over non-depolarizing relaxants as the
ris.k of vom iting and re gurgitation of stomach
contents is less with it.

## Operation Theatre

_Bask req11irements of general operation theatre_ with
equipment for general anaesthesia and facilities to
deal with the cardiorespiratory emergency situalions
should be available in eye operation theatre.

_**Equipme11t for ophthalmic operation theatre**_ should
incl1Ude:

- Operating microscope (Fig. 26.5A),

- Ophthalmic operating table and surgeon's chair,

- Ophthalmic cryo unit (Fig. 26.58),

- Wet-field bipolar cautery (Fig. 26.SC),

- Electrolysis machine,

- Electromagne ti c unit for removal of intraocular
foreign bodies,

- Virrectomy unit (Fig. 26.5D), and

- Phacoemulsification machi ne (Fig. 26.5£) for
modern cataract surgery.

## Ciphthalmic Instruments

Commonly used ophthalmic instruments can be
grouped as under:

I. **Lid speculums**
Three types of speculums _are_ in use:

**l.** _Un_ _i11ersa/ metalllceyespeculum(Fig._ 26.6). It is called
universal eye speculum because it can be used for
both eyes, i.e. right as _well_ as left. It has two limbs
and a spring mechanism with a screw ro adjust the
limbs. Its blades are open.

'

_C. Wet-field bipolar cautery; D, Vitrectomy unit; E, Phacoemulsification machine_

2. _Eye speculum_ _with guard or with_ _solid blades_
(Fig. 26. 7). It retracts the lids and its solid blades
keep the lashes away from the field of operation.

3. _Wire speculum_ (Fig. 26.8). It is very light and causes
mjnimal pressure on the eyeball. IL is also universal. Fig. **26.8** _Wire speculum_

**616** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

_**Uses.**_ Eye speculums are used to keep the lids apart
during:

    - lntraocular operations such as cataract surgery,
glaucoma surgery and keratoplasty.

    - Extraocular surgery, e.g. squint surgery, pterygium
surgery.

    - Enucleation and evisceration operation.

    - Removal of conjunctiva! and corneal foreign
bodies.

    - Cauterization of corneal ulcer.

    - Exa m in ation of th e eye in a pa tie nt w ith
blepharospasm.

II. **Forceps**
Many kinds of forceps are available for different
purposes. A few common ones are mentioned here.
1. _Plain forceps_ (Fig. 26.9). It is simple forcep wi thout
any teeth. Serrations (either horizontal or vertical)
are present n ea r th e tip. _**Uses:**_ (i) To hold th e
conjunctiva during any surgical procedure. (ii) To tie
sutures. (iii) To hold scleral flap in trabeculectomy.
(iv) To hold skin during eyelid surgery. (v) To hold
nasal mucosal flaps and lacrimal sac flaps in DCR
operation.

2. _Globe fixation forceps_ (Fig. 26.10). It has 2 x 3 or 3
x 4 teeth at the tip. Tt is applied near the limbus to
hold the conjunctiva and episcleral tissue together.
_Uses:_ (i) To fi x the eyebalJ during operations on the
eyeball. (ii) To hold the eyeball dmingforced-duction
test.

3. _Superior rectu.s holding forceps_ (Fig. 26.11). It is a
toothed forceps (1 x 2 teeth) with S-shaped double
curve near the tip. _**Uses:**_ It is used to hold the superior
rectus muscle while passing a bridle suture under it;
to stabilize the eyeball during any operation such as
cataract surgery, glaucoma surgery, corneal surgery,
etc.

4. _Corneo-scleralforceps._ These are available in many
shapes and designs. Commonly used are:

_Calibri forceps_ (Fig. 26. 12A) and _Lim's force_ _ps_
(Fig. 26.12B). These are the forceps with ve11 [1] fine teeth
(1 x 2) at the tip. _**Use:**_ These are used to hold the
cornea or scleral e dge (of incision) for suturing
during cataract, glaucoma, repair of corneal and/ or
scleral tears and keratoplasty operations.

**m.:::::~§~~~Tu~t..=:..~111!:':_~_=--:_..~**

_B, Lim's forceps_

5. _lrisforceps_ (Fig. 26.13). These are small a nd
delicate forceps having fine 1 x 2 teeth on the i1mer
side of the limbs. These are also available in various
shapes and designs. _Use:_ These are used to catch the
iris for the purpose of iridectomy during operations
for cataract, glaucoma, optical iridecto my a nd
excision for iris prolapse, tumo urs an d entangled
foreign bodies.

6. _Arm ga's intracapsular (capsule holding) forceps_
(Fig. 26.14). lntracapsular forceps have a cup on the
inner side of the tip of each limb. The margins of the
cup are very smooth which do not damage the lens
capsule when applied. _**Use:**_ _Not used nowadays._ In
the era of intracapsular cataract extraction (ICCE),
it was used to ho ld the lens capsule (usually at 6
O'clock position) during capsule forceps method
of lens delivery in intracapsular cataract extraction
and to grasp and remove the capsular remnants after
accidental extra.capsular lens exn·action.

_forceps_

7. _Epilationforceps_ (Fig. 26.15). These are small stout

forceps with blunt and flat ends. _Uses:_ These are used
to epilate the cilia in trichiasis and stye, to remove
cilia after electrolysis and cryolysis and to remove
cilia lodged in the punctum.

8. _Artery (lw_ _emostalic) Jorceps_ (Fig. 26.16). It is a
blunt-tipped stout forceps having a scissors-like
configuration. It has multiple straight grooves (at
right angle lO the limbs) near the tip and a locking
mechanism near the ringed end. These _are_ available
in large, medium and small size. The small-sized
artery forceps, _also_ called as _mosquito arte,y forceps_
are more commonly used in oph thalmology. These
can be straight or with curved ends. _Uses:_ (i) To catch
the bleeding vessels during opera ti ons of the lids and
lacrimal sac. (ii) To hold the skin and muscle stay
sutures. (iii) To hold small 'pea-nut' gauze pellets
for blunt dissection in lacrimal sac surgery and other
extraocular surgery. (iv) To hold gauze pieces while
packing the socket after enucleation or exenteration
operation.

Ill. **Hooks and retractors**

L. _Lens expressor (hook)_ (Fig. 26.17). It is a flat metal
handle with a rounded curve at one end. Tip of
the curve is knob bed. The plane of the handle is
at right angle to the curvat ure of the hook. _**Uses:**_ (i)
To express the nucleus in extraca psular cataract
extraction. (ii) It can also be used as mu scle hook
if the latter is not available. (iii) Also used along
with wire vectis to extract out the dislocated lens.
{iv) 1n the era of ICCE, it was used to apply pressure
on the limbus at the 6 O'clock position during the
delivery of lens in intracapsu lar cataract extraction
with Smith's (tumbling) and caps ule forceps
techniques.

2. _Muscle (stmbismus) hook_ (Fig. 26.18). It is similar
to the lens expressor in appearance but has a blunt
guarding knob at the end to prevent muscle slip page.
The plane of the hand le is the same as that of the
curvature of the hook. _Uses:_ {i) It is used to engage
the extraocular muscles during surgery for squint,
enucleation, and retinal detachment. (ii) In the
absence oflens expressor, it may be used in its place.

3. _Desmorre's retractor_ (Pig. 26.J 9). It is a saddleshaped instrument folded on itself at one end. It
is available in two sizes: small (paediatric) and
large (adult). _Use:_ It is used to retract the lids
during exami nation of the eyeball in cases of
blepharospasm in children, in cases with marked
swelling and ecchymosis, removal of corneoscleral
su tures, remova l of corneal foreign body and
for double eversion of upper lid to examine the
superior forni.x. _Advantages._ Allows continuous
adjusLment of the lids and width of the palpebral
aperture.

_Disad11a11tages._ It is not self-retaining, so an assistant

is needed to hold it.
4. _Cat's paw lacrimal wowul retractor(Fig._ 26.20). It is a

fork-like instrument with the terminals bent inward.

_Use:_ It i used to retract the skin during lacrimal sac
and lid surgery.

5. _Self-retaini11g lacrlmal wound (Muller's) retractor_
(Fig. 26.21). It is made up of two limbs with three
curved pins on each for engaging the edges of the
skin incision. The limbs are kept in a retracted
position with the help of a fixing screw. _Use:_ It is used
to retract the skin during surgery on the lacrimal sac
(e.g. OCT or OCR).

_{Muller's) retractor_

6. _lris retractor(Fig._ 26.22). It consists of a handle with
a curved blade (which co nforms to the pupillary
margin) at one end. Its edges and corners are
rounded so as not to damage either the iris or the
lens capsule. _Use:_ To retract the upper edge of pupil
in cryoextraction technique of ICCE and also to
aspirate the lens matter from behind the iris at 12
O'clock position in ECCE.

**IV. Needle holders**
I. _Spri_ _11g actio11_ _(Barraquer's_ _type) needle_ _holder_
(Fig. 26.23). These are available in various sizes with
straight or curved tips, in different shapes and may

be with or without locking system. The jaws of the
needle holder are finely serrated to hold the fin e
needles firmly. _Use:_ Spring type needle holders are
used fo r passing sutures in the conjunctiva, cornea,
sclera and extraocular muscles.

2. _Castrouiejo's_ _needle holder_ (Fig. 26.24). It is a
medium-sized spring action needle holder wilh a
S-shaped locking system. _**Use:**_ It is generally used
in extraocular surgery, e.g. conjunctiva! suturing,
squint surgery, etc. It can also be used for inrraocular
surgery.

3. _Arruga's,_ _Steven, Silcock's and kelt needle holder_
(Fig. 26.25). These are large needle holders and all
are of similar type with slight model differences. The
upper shank of these needle holders has a Hat and
broad plate to accommodate the surgeon's thumb.
These are available with and wid1out locking device.

_lhe:_ These a re very commonly used in lid surgery and
also fo r passing superior rectus suture.

**V. Callipers and rules**

I. _Cnstrouiejo calliper_ (Fig. 26.26). It is a dividerlike instrumen t, to one arm of which is attached

lo a graduated scale (in mm ). Its other arm can be
moved by a screw over the scale. _**Use:**_ It is used to
take measurements d uring squin t, ptosis, retinal
detachment and pars plana vitrectomy surgery. It is
also used to measure corneal diameter and visible
horizo ntal iris diame ter.

2. _Melllllic rule:_ It is used as a scale for the Castroviejo
call iper for exact measurements and to measure tlhe
palpebral apem1re widd1.

**VI. Knives and knife-needles**

I . _Vo_ _n Grae/e's knife_ (Fig. 26.27). IL is a long, narrow,
thin and straight blade with a sharp tip and cuttiing
edge on one side. _Uses:_ It is not used present ly,
and is described here only for the historical value of
an instrument which has been extensively used in
the past (for making an ab-interno corneoscleral
incision during cataract surgery and for iridectonny
operation, four dot iridectomy operation in patients
with iris bombe form ation, and for ma king: a
puncture in pars plana area during lensectomy and
vitrectomy operation).

2. _Keratomes_ (Fig. 26.28). A keratome has a th in
diamond-shaped blade with a sharp apex and two
cutting edges. Straight as well as curved keratomes
are available in various sizes (2.8 mm, 3 mm, 3.5 mm,
5.5 mm). Presently disposable curved keratomes
are more commonly used. _**Use:**_ Keratomes are us,ed
to make valvul ar corneal incisions fo r entry into
the anterior chamber fo r all modern techniques of
cataract extraction viz. phacoemulsi£ica tion, SICS
and even conventional ECCE and other intraocul1ar
surgeries, e.g. itidectomies and paracentesis.

3. _Paracentesis needle_ (Fig. 26.29). It is a small lancetshaped needle with sharp cutting edges resembling
in a ppearance to a small keratome. It has got· a
guard to prevent in advertent injury to the deeper
structures. _Use:_ It is _not used presently,_ previously,
it was used for paracentesis and to make very small

corneoscleral incisions. Presently used alternative
Lo Lhe paracentesis needle are 15 degrees side port
blade _(See_ Fig. 26.31) and MVRorV lance blade _(See_

4. _1ooke's knife_ (Fig. 26.30). It has a short nat blade
with a semicircular blunt dissecting edge, wh ich

is bevelJed on both the surfaces like a chisel. _Uses:_
(i) It ca n be used to separate the co njun ctiva
an d s ub conjunctiva! tiss ue from the sclera
and limbus when limbal based fl ap is made for
trabeculectom y surgery. (ii) It can also be used ro
separate partial thickness lameUae of sclera during
trabeculectomy. (iii) To separate pterygium head
or limbal dermoid from the underlying corneal
la mellae. (iv) To separate cornea l lamellae in
lamellar kerato plasty.

Presently, it is sparingly used a nd has been
replaced by the crescent knife _(See_ Fig. 26.37).

5. 15° _side port entry_ _blade_ (Fig. 26.3 1). It is a fine
straight knife with a sharp pointed tip and cutting
edge on one side. _Use._ It is used to make a small
valvular clear corneal incision (commonly called as
_side port incision)_ in phacoemulsification and other
intraocular surgeries including pars plana vitrectomy.

6. _MVRor \flance_ _blade (Fig._ 26.32). lt is a fine straight
buL triangular knife similar to 15° side port entry
blade but with cutting edges on both sides. _Use:_ Its
uses are similar to 15° side port entry blade.

7. _Cystotome or capsulotome_ (Fig. 26.33). It is a small
needle knife with a bent Lip which is sharp on
both the ed ges. Presently, disposable cystotome
is prepa red by bending the dis posable 26 gauge
or 30 gauge hypodermic needle. _Use:_ It is used for
doing anterior capsulotomy or capsul orhexis during
extracapsular cataract extraction.

8. _Foreign body spud_ (Fig. 26.34). It has a small, stout
and Oat blatde with blunt tip and edges on both sides.

_Use:_ It is used to remove corneal foreign body.

9. _Razor bludefragment with blade holder._ Razor blade
fragment holder (Fig. 26.35) is designed to hold the
razor blade fragment firmly in its jaws and has a
locking device. The razor blade fragments broken to
a uniform :size and shape have the sharpest possible
me tal ed ge with an absolute point. Prese ntl y,
presteriliz,ed razor blade fragments mounted on a
disposable plastic handle (Fig. 26.36) are also being
preferred. _Use:_ his the most commonly used cutting
device for making incisions in cataract, glaucoma,
keratoplasty, sclerotomy, pterygium and many other
operations.

**Fi~I- 26.35** _Razor blade fragment holder_

10. _Crescent knife_ ( Fig. 26.37). It is blunt-lipped,
bevel up k nife having cut-splitting action at the
tip and at both sides. Its blade is curved and either
m ounted on a pla stic ha ndle (disposable) or
can be fix,ed with metallic handle. _Use:_ It is used
(i) To make tunn el in cis ion in the sclera and
cornea for phacoe mulsifi cation, manual small
incision cataract surgery (SICS), and sutu reless
trabeculectomy. (ii) To separate the conjunc tiva and
subconjunictival tissue from the sclera and limbus
when Umbal-based flap is made for trabeculectomy
surgety. (i'ii) It can also be used to separate partial
thickness lamellae of sclera during trabeculectomy.
(iv) To separate pterygium head or limbal dermoid

from the underlying corneal lamellae. _(_ v) To separate
corneal lamellae in lamellar keratoplasty.

I 1. _Bard-Parker's ha,idle_ (Fig. 26.38) named after
Charles Russel Bard and Morgan Parker, this handle
is available indifferent sizes. Also known as BP

handle, it is light and affords a firm grip. Blade of
different sizes can be fitted onto a groove at the tip of
the handle for making the desired incision.

12. _Extension keratome_ (Fig. 26.39) similar to sharp
keratome, it has 1:\-vo sharp edges, but the tip is blunt
and rounded. It is used to extend size of corneal
incision, when needed.

13. _Wright's (11eedle) knife_ (Fig. 26.40} a spear-shaped
knife with an eye in middle to thread the silicone
and fascia lata sling in ptosis surgery.

**VII. Scissors**
l. _Plai11_ _straight_ _scissors (ringed)_ (Fig. 26.41). It is a fine
pointed scissors with straight sharp cutting blades.
_Use:_ It is used to cut conjunctival sutures, eyelashes,
and muscles.

2. _Plaili curued scissors (ri11ged)_ (Fig. 26.42). It is a fine
pointed scissors wi th curved, sharp cutting blades.

_Use:_ It is used to cut and undermine conjunctiva in
various operations and to undermine skin during
operations on lids and lacrimal sac.

3. _Tenotomy scissors or strabismu.s scissors_ (Fig. 26.43).
They are plain straight or curved scissors with blunt
ends. _Uses:_ (i) To cut the extraocular muscles during
squint surgery and enu cleation operation. (ii) To
separate the delicate tissues without damaging the
surround ing area in oculoplastic operations and
squint surgery.

4. _Corneal scissors_ _or section enlarg_ _i11g_ _scissors_
(Fig. 26.44). They are fine curved scissors. Their
cutting blades are kept apa rt by spring action .
They are available in various shapes and sizes. The

uni versal corn eal scissors can be used for both
sides while right and left curved corneal scissors
are separated fo r the two sides. _Uses:_ (i) These are
used to enlarge corneal or corneoscleral incision
fo r conventional intra capsular and extracapsular
cataract extraction techniques (sparingly perfo rmed
procedures nowadays) of cataract surgery. (ii) To
enlarge corneal incision in keratoplasty operation.
(iii) To cut the scleral an d tra becular tissue in
trabecuJectomy. (iv) It can also be used for cutting
and undermining conjunctiva in various operations.

_**A,**_ _CuNed; B, Angled_

5. _de Wecker_ _'s scissors_ (Fig. 26.45). Th ey a re fine
scissors with small blades directed at right angles to
the arms. The blades arc kept apart, making V-shape,
by spring action. _Use:_ It is used to perform iridectomy,
iridotomy and to cut the prolapsed form vitreous and
pupiUary membrane.

6. _Spring scissors (Westcott's)_ (Fig. 26.46). They are
stout s cissors avai lable with straight or c u rved
blades wid1 sharp or blunt tips. The blades are kept
apart by spring action. _Use:_ Th ey are used as a handy
alternative to plain straight and plain curved ringed
scissors for cu rring and und e rmining conjunc Liva in
various operations and to cut sutures.

7. _Van11as scissors_ (Fig. 26.47). These are ve ry fine
delicate scissors with sma ll c utting blad es kept
apart by spring action. The blades may be straight
or c urve d. _Uses:_ (i) These a re used for c utting
a nterior capsule of the lens in extracapsular surgery
a nd for cutting 10-0 nylon sutures. (ii) For c utting
in ner scleral flap in trabecul ectomy. (iii) For doing
pupi llary sphin cterotom y. (iv) For pe rformi ng
iridectomy. (v) .For cutting pupillary membrane.

8. _E1111cleation scissors_ (Fig. 26.48). They are large, stout
and strong scissors having curved sh arp blades with
blunt ends. _Use:_ They are used to cut the optic nerve
during enucleation operation.

**VIII. Clamps**
I. _lid clamp orentropion clamp_ (Fig. 26.49). It consists
of a O-shaped plate opposed by a U-shape rim, which
when tightened with the help of a screw, clamps the
tissues. Two clamps are req uired; one can be used

for right upper and left lower lid and the second for
right lower and left upper lid. While applying the lid
clamp, the plate is kept towards the conjunctiva) side,
the rim on the skin side, and the handle is always
situated on the temporal side.
_Advantage over lid spawla:_ It is a self-retain ing
instrument and does not need an assistant to hold.
_Disadvantages:_ (i) Operative field is less. (ii) Pressure
necrosis can occur if fitted Lightly. _Uses:_ It is used in
lid surgery, e.g. entropion, and ectropion corrections.
It protects the eyeball, suppo rts the lid tissue and
provides haemostasis during surgery.

2. _Chnlazion clnmp_ (Fig. 26.50). It consists of two limbs
like a forceps, which can be clamped with the h elp of
a screw. The tip of one limb is flattened in the form of
round disc while d1e tip of the other a rm has a small
circular ring. Usually, the flat disc is applied on the
skin side a nd ring on the conjunctiva) side of th e
chala7Jon. However, rarely when the chalazion points
towards the skin, the ring side is applied to the skin.
_Use:_ To fix the chalazion and achieve haemostasis
during the incision a nd curettage operation.

3. _Ptosis clamp_ (Fig. 26.5 l). It is like a forceps with
I-shaped ends having internal serrations. The clamp
has a locking mechanism. _Use:_ To hold levator
palpcbrac superioris muscle during ptosis surgery.

**IX. Additional instruments for cataract surgery**

I . _Lens spatula_ (Fig. 26.52). It is a flat metallic handle
wit h tiny spoon-sh aped e nds. It is used to apply

**622** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

counter-pressure at 12 O'clock position d uri ng
expression of nucleus in extra ca psular cataract
extraction. In the era of TCCE, it was u sed in a
similar way to extract cataractous lens in Smith's
technique.

2. _Wire vectis_ (Fig. 26.53). It is a wire loop attached to a
metallic handle. _Use:_ It is used to remove subluxated
lens and anteriorly dislocated lens in ICCE technique
and the nucleus in ECCE technique.

3. _Irrigating wire vectis_ (Fig. 26.54). It is a modified
vectis in which the loop is made of a thick hollow
wire. The anterior end of the loop has three 0.3 mm
openings. The posterior end of the loop is continuous
with a hollow handle. The posterior end of the hollow
handle has a hub similar to that of a hypodermic
needle to which is attached a syringe or infusion
set. The size of the loop of the vecris is variable.
In commonly used wire vectis, the loop is 4 mm in
width and about 8-9 mm in length. The sup erior
surface of th e lo op has a slight co ncav ity to
accommodate the lens nucleus. _**Use:**_ Irrigating wire
vectis is most commonly used to deliver nucleus in

the manual small incision cataract surgery (SICS)
and in conventional ECCE by hydro expression or
visco expression technique.

**4 .** _**Two**_ _**-way**_ _**irrigation**_ _**and**_ _**as**_ _**piration**_ _**cannula**_
(Fig. 26.55). lt has two openings, one on tip of
cannula fo r irrigation and one on anterior surface

for aspiration of lens matter or other contents. At
its posterior end, there is one hub anteri or to that
of hypodermic needle for attachment to TV line for
iJTigation. At the other side, there is a cannula with
a plastic tubing and another hub for attachment to
a syringe or other source of vacuum for aspiration.
It is available in various designs, commonly used
are Simcoe's classical or reverse cannula. _Uses:_ (i)
For irrigation and aspiration of the lens matter in
extracapsular cataract extraction. (ii) Aspiration of
hyphaema.

5. _Iris repositor(Fig._ 26.56). ft consists ofa delicate, flat,
malleable, straight or bent blade with blunt edges and
tip attached to a handle. _Uses:_ (i) To reposit the iris in

the anterior chamber in any ino·aocular surge1y.(ii)
To break synechiae at the pupillaiy margin.

6. _Capsulorhexisforceps._ It is a very fine tipped delicate
forceps with small curved tips (Fig. 26.57) to hold the
anterior lens capsule during Continuous curvili near
capsulorrhexis, wh ile avoiding damage to oth er
intraocular structures.

**X. Additional instruments for intraocular lensimplantation**
For IOL implantation, the cataract surgery set should
contain the following basic additional instruments:

l. _lOL holdingforceps(Fig._ 26.58). It is a spring action
forceps with short, blunt and curved blade shaving
smooth edges and tips with platform (no teeth or
serrations). _Use:To_ hold optic of non-foldable PMMA
IOL during implantation.

2. _Kelma1i-McPherso11forceps_ (Fig. 26.59). These
are fi ne forceps \o\rith bent limbs. _Uses:_ (i) To hold
th e sup erior haptic of IOL during its placement.

(ii) To tear-off the anterior capsuJar flap in ECCE.
(iii) Can be used for suture tying.

3. _Sinskeyhookor IOLdialer (Fig._ 26.60). It is a fine but
stout instrument with a bent tip. The tip engages the
dialing holes of the IOL. _**Uses:**_ {i) It is used to dial the
PMMA non-foldable lOL for proper positioning in the
capsular bag or ciliary sulcus. (ii) It can also be used

to manipulate the nucleus in phacoemulsifjcation
surgery. Nucleus manipulation may be in the form
of nucleus rotation in the capsuJar bag, cracking of
the nucleus and feeding of the nuclear fragments
into the phaco tip.

Flg. **26.60** _Sinskey hook or IOL dialer_

4. _Hydrodissection ca,mula_ (Fig. 26.61). It is a single
bore 25G, 27G or 30G cannula with a 45° angulation
at about 10 co 12 mm from the free end. The tip at the
free end can be flattened or bevelled. _Use:_ It is used
to perform hydro dissection (separation of co rtex
from the posterior capsule) and hydrodelineation
(sepa ra tion of co rtex from th e nuc leu s) in

phacoemulsification and manual SICS. This cannula
is attached to the syringe carrying irrigating fluid.
For hydrodissection its tip is introduced beneath
the anterior capsular margin after capsulorhexis and
fluid is injected to obtain subcapsular dissection.

5. _Chopper_ {Fig. 26.62). The chopper is a fine
instrument resembling sinskey hook in shape. The
inner edge of the bent tip is cutting and may have
different angles. _Use:_ It is used to split or chop the
nucleus into s maller pieces and also for nu clear
manipulation in phacoemulsification surgery.

6. _Ring capsule polisher or posterior capsule polishing_
_curette_ (Fig. 26.63). It consists of a long handle and

a bent slender neck. The tip of the instrument has a
tiny circular ring. _Use:_ Jt is used to clear and polish
the posterior lens capsule co make it more clear in
the extracapsular cataract surgery. It is specially
used when a plaque or sticky cortex is adhered to
the posterior capsule.

_polishing curette_

**XI. Additional instruments for glaucoma surgeryl.** Selem/ _punch_ (Fig. 26.64). It is similar in shape to
corneal scissors with spring action mechanism. Its

2. _Kelley's punch_ (Fig. 26.65). It consists of two blades.

which slides with a spring mechanism around the

inner blade. While in use during trabeculectomy

ti ss ue in conventional as well as s uture less

**XII. Additional instruments for lid surgeryl.** _Chalazionscoop_ {Fig. 26.66). ft has a small cup with
sharp margins attached to a narrow handle. _Use:_ To

2. _Lid spaw la_ (Fig. 26.67). It is a simple metal plate
having slightly convex surfaces at either end. _Use:_

**XIII. Additional instruments for lacrimal sac andorbital surgery**

1. _Punct_ _um dilator_ _(Nettles/zip 's)_ (Fig. 26.68). lt
has a cylindrical corrugated metal handle with
a conical pointed tip. _Use:_ To dilate the punctum
and canaliculus during sy ringin g, probing,

2. _Lncrimal probes (Bowmam's)_ (Fig. 26.69). These are

during DCT and DCR operations.

3. _Lacrimal cannula_ (Fig. 26.70). It is a long curved

hypodermic needle with blunt tip. _Use:_ (i) For
syringing the lacrimal passages. (ii) As AC cannula
for putting air, tryphan blue dye, pilocarpine and
balanced salt solution in the anterior chamber
during intraocular surgety.

**4.** _BonepuncJ,_ (Fig. 26.71). It consists of a stout spring
handle and two blades attached at right angle. The
upper blade has a small hole with a sharp cutting
edge. The lower blade has a cup-like depression.
_Use:_ It is used to enlarge the bony opening dtuing
OCR operation by punching the bone from margins
of the opening. Carelessness during this step can
cause accidental damage to the nasal mucosa and
the nasal septwn.

5. _Chisel_ (Fig. 26.72). It consists of a blade having
a sharp-cutting straight edge with one surface
bevelled. It has a lo ng and stout handle. _Use:_ To cut
the bone during OCR and or bitotomy operations.

6. _Hammer_ (Fig. 26.73). It is a small steel hammer
attached to a corrugated handle. _Use:_ To hammer
the chisel during OCR and orbitotomy operations.

7. _Lacrimal sac dissector and curene_ (Fig. 26.74). It is a
cylindrical instrument, one end of which is a blunttipped dissector and the other end is curetted. _Use:_ ln
lacrimal sac surgery.

8. _Bone gouge_ (Fig. 26.75). It consists ofa stout metallic
handle, one end of which is longitudinally scooped.
The edges of the scoop are sharp. _Use:_ To smoothen
the irregularly cut margins of the bone by nibbling
small projecting bone and in DCR operation and in
orbitotomy operation.

9. _Freer's periosteum elevator(Fig._ 26. 76). This doubleended instrument typically has a rounded handle
with two slightly curved, tear-drop tips.
_Uses:_ rt can be used as a multipurpose tool in many
pmcedures although it is primarily used for blunt

debulking and lifting periosteum from bones in
confined areas. It is used _in_ endoscopic septoplasty
and DCR. It is also handy in any proced ure that
involves dissecting soft tissue cartilage or bone.

IO. _**Bone drill.**_ (Fig. 26. 77) Comprises of a burr head
and a power cord connectingitto a motorized rotating
unit and a foot pedal to control its functioning.
_**Uses are:**_ To create the ostium in the DCR surgeries
especially endon asal DCR, and to create holes in
the bones, in cases of orbitotomies for placement of
fixation wires.

**XIV. Additional instruments for enucleation andeviscerationJ.** _**Optic 11ervegui.de(enucleatio11**_ _**spoo11) (Fig.**_ 26.78).It is
a spoon-shaped instrument with a central cleavage.
_**Use:**_ To engage the optic nerve during enucleation.

**2.** _**Evisceratio11**_ _**spatula**_ (Fig. 26.79). It consists of a
small but stout rectangular blade with slightly convex
surface and blunt edges attached to a handle. _Use:_ To
separate out the uveal tissue from the sclera during
evisceration operation.

3. _Evisceration curette_ (Fig. 26.80 ). It consists of an oval
or rounded shallow cup with blunt margins attached
to a stout handle. _Use:_ To curette out the intraocular
contents during evisceration operation.

## Ophthalmic Surgical Devices

During practical examination, the students may

common ophthalmic surgical devices used during
various ocular surgeries. These are described briefly:

**Surgical devices used in cataract surgeryJ.** _Ocular viscoelastic d_ _evices_ _(OVDs),_ used in
intraocular surgery, are available in vials and
prefilled syringes (PFs). OVDs in PFs are packed in
box (Fig. 26.81), and are preferred over vial packs
and so more commonly used. Various types of OVDs
are described on page 477.

Ophthalmic Viscosurgical Device

2. _Jntraomlar lenses (JOLs)_ used in cataract surgery
(Fig. 26.82) are described on page 213

used in patients wilh zonular dialysis and subluxated
lenses. These work on the principle of traction,
expansion and stabilization of the capsular bag.
Commonly used are Marcher CTRs (Fig. 26.83A) and
Cionni CTRs (Fig. 26.83B)

## Operation Theatre Layout

1 PCB has laid down certain specific guidelines
for layout of an oph thalmic operation theatre
(OT) to reduce the occurrence of postoperative
endophthalmitis. The main features include:

7. _Butterfly cartridge a_ _11d intraocula r le11s injeclor._ The
co mmonly used foldable TOL injector comprises
a butterfly shaped cartridge (Fig. 26.87 A) and an
injector hand piece (Fig. 26.87B). The cartridge
comprises a loading chamber adapted to hold a
folldable intraocular lens, an injection chamber, and
a compression chamber. The injector hand piece is
connected with the cartridge loaded with IOL. The
tip of injection chamber is introduced in the anterior
chamber of eye through d1e corneal incision and the
piston of hand piece is pushed which releases the
IOL through the aperture of the injection chamber
irnto me eye.

## Ophthalmic Operation Theatre

_SepnratP 011/Jtlwlmlc t/Jeatre complex_ for ophthalmic
procedures sho uld be there in a mu ltispeciality
hospital.
_Operation T/Jeatre (OT) complex to be arranged in four_
_levels_ as below:

 - _Level I:_ changing room- shoes plus clothes plus
record keeping.

 - _Level fl:_ OT clothes plus caps and masks to be worn
both by the patients and the staff plus scrub room.

 - _Level lll_ should be fumigated areas where surgeries
can be performed.

 - _Level IV_ should be for disposal plus insLruments
maintenance.

- _Laminar air flow_ from centre of the OT complex
towards outer areas to avoid entry of the air-borne
pathogens.

- _Air entry from air conditioners_ to be cleaned once
a month and filtered source.

- _Separate set of instruments_ to be used in each
patient and proper autoclaving to be done after
every use.

## Preparation Of Operation

## Theatre

- Mopping of floor with disinfectant.

- Cleaning of all operating surfaces and trolleys with
carbolic acid 10% solution followed by distilled
water every day.

- All linen to be washed, dried and autoclaved.

## Sterilization, Disinfection And

## Fumigation

## Sterilization And Disinfection

_**Sterilization**_ is a process which kills or removes all
micro-organisms including bacterial spores from
an article, surface or medium (inanimate objects).
It must be differentiated from _**disi11fectio1i**_ which
destroys pathogenic micro-organisms but does not
kill or remove spores.

Sterilization can be accomplished by _physical_
_agents (viz._ sunlight, heat, filtration and radiation)
and _chemical agents_ (such as alcohols, aJdehydes,
halogens, phenol, surface acting agents and gases).

**Methods of sterilisationA.** _**Heat sterilization**_
Both dry a nd moist heat can be used.

**Dry heat sterilization methods**

1. _Flaming_ refers to direct e>.1Josure of the instrument
to flame of a burner/spirit lamp. It eliminates most
of microbes from the of instrument. It can be used to
sterilize points of forceps, hypodermic needles, tips

of AC cannula and scraping spatulas. The instrument
is held in a bunsen flame tiU it becomes red hot.
2. _Incineration._ It is used to destroy soiled dressings,
beddings aind pathological materials.
3. _Hot air oven._ It is the mo st commonly used
method of sterilization by dry hear. It kills bacteria,
spores and! viruses. This method is employed to
sterilize instruments like forceps, scissors, scalpels,
glass syringes, glassware, etc. The article must be
double-wrapped and kept at 150°C for l ho ur or

rno•c for 20 minutes.

**Moist heat sterilization methods**

I. _Boiling._ H kills b acteria and viruses. Heavier
metallic ophthalmic instruments, e.g. Bard-Parker
handles, lid guards, etc. can be sterilized by boiling in
water fo r 30 minutes. This method, however, blunts
the cutting instrumems.
2. _Steaming._ It kills most bacteria and viruses, but not
spores. The instruments are placed on a shelf above
the level ohvater and steamed for about 30 minutes.
Mose of the metallic instruments, e.g. scissors and
knives can be sterilized by this method.
3 . _Steam_ _with formaldehyde._ Steam h as been

used traditionally for its microbicidal activity.
Latest evollution in this method involves us ing
formaldehyde vapours along with steam as it helps
in killing spores also.
4. _Autoclaving (steam_ _under pressure)._ It is the most
widely used method for sterilization. It is based
on the principle that at boiling point of water, the
vapour pressure equates the atmospheric pressure.
So, if the pressure is increased, boiling point tends
to rise, wh ich increases the penetrating power of
the steam.

Autoclaving at 134°C at 34 psi preserve fo r 3
minutes or 1121 •c under 15 lb/in [2 ] pressure for 20 min.
or at I J 6°C under IO lb/in [2 ] pressure for 40 min. kills
bacteria, spores and viruses. This method is suitable
for sterilizin1g various instruments, linen, glasswares,
rubber goods, gowns, towels, gloves dressings and
eye drops.

In autoclaving, sterilization occurs due to the
latenc heat which is given out when steam condenses
to water. Therefore, to sterilize effectively the steam
must come into direct contact with the surface to be
sterilized.

Autoclave is a machine available in varying sizes
and types and uses steam and high pressure to kill
microbe and spores. Time needed for sterilization
depends on the temperature and pressure achieved
by the machine. Table top autoclaves are compact
machines used to autoclave instruments in short
time.

**628** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

**B.** _**Chemical sterilization**_
Chemical steriJization agents include:
_**Alkylating agents**_

    - Formaldehyde

    - Glutaraldehyde

    - Ethylene oxide
_**Oxidizing agents**_

    - Peracetic acid

    - Hydrogen peroxide

    - Ozone

    - Chlorine dioxide.

1. Savlon. lt comprises of cetavlon or cetrimide and
chlorhexidine. Cetavlon is a surface active agent and
chlorhexidine is a phenol. It is active against most
Gram-positive organisms. It is used for cleaning/
preparation of skin. Scissors, catheters, knives, etc.
may also be sterilized with it.

2. Spirit (95% _alcohol)._ lt kills bacteria and spores, but
not viruses. It is mostly used with savlon.
3. MeLhylated spirit. It is 70% isopropyl alcohol. Schiotz
tonometer can be sterilized by it.

**4. Formaldehyde**
i. _Formalin chamber._ About 10% solution of formalin

in has a marked bactericidal, sporicidal and some
viricidal activity. It is suitable fo r cryoextractor
probes and o phthalmosco py lenses and h eat
sensitive instruments.
ii. _Formaldehyde gas._ It may be used for fu migating

wards, sick rooms and laboratories. But this gas is
irritant and toxic when inhaled.
5. Glutaraldehyde (2% ). It is available as 'Cidex' solution.
It has a special activity against tubercle bacilli, fungi
and viruses. It is mostly used for sterilising endoscopes
because it has no damaging effect on the lenses. It can
be safely used for catheters, face-masks, anaesthetic
tubes and metal instruments. However, it is not suitable
for silicone tubing. It is specially used to sterilise sharp
instruments, as it does not affect the sharpness. In
three hours, the instruments are free of pathogens and
spores. Instruments should be thoroughJywashed with
sterile distilled water before use.
**6. Hydrogen peroxide.** About 3% solution of H20 2 is
used for sterilisation of applanation tonometers,
prisms and ophthalmoscopy lenses. It is especially
active against AIDS and herpes viruses.

7. **Ethylene oxide gas:** Ethylene oxide (ETO) is a highly
inflammable gas and is usually mixed with an inert
gas like nitrogen or carbon dioxide. It denatures the
protein molecules by aJkylation in which hydrogen
atom is replaced by a hydroxyethyl radical with in
the protein molecul e. When a sufficie nt number
of proteins within molecules of micro-organisms

ar,e alkylated, death ensues. The process kills all
rnicro-organisms including tubercular bacilli, other
bacteria, spores and viruses.

_U$es:_ ETO steri lizatio n is a low te mperature
sterilization method. lt is used to sterilize medical
anid pharmaceutical products that cannot support
conventional high temperature steam sterilization.
Goniotomy lenses, indirect ophth almoscopy
lenses, OCR tub ings, vit rectomy cutters, lens
probes, diathermy leads, most disposable items and
cr yoprobes can be sterilized with it.
_Procedure._ The articles to be sterili zed are put in
the ETO chamber which is then heated up to 4060l0C. Steam is introduced to rehumidi fy the load.
(Moisture enhances diffusion ofETO). The ETO gas
(400- 1000 mg/ I space) is then introduced from a
cylinder. The gas is removed and the chamber and
its: contents are flushed with filtered air. Sterilization
time may vary from 1 to 12 hours depending upon
th e absorbency of the load, temperature and air
ex.change rate.
8. Acetone. Use of aceto ne is a quick and cheap

m ethod of sterilising instruments. fnstruments
should be kept in acetone for 5 minutes and then
thoroughly washed with sterile water before use.

**C.** _**Plasma sterilization:**_
Plasma is the 4th state of matter and usually consists
of reactive cloud of ions, electrons, free radical and
other neutral species. The sterrad process is a plasma
system that uses hydrogen peroxide as the source of
active species. To ensure adequacy of this method,
Hp 2 must diffuse to all parts of the load. Packaging

material and soiling may decrease efficiency of this
system.

**D ..** _**Radiation sterilization**_

1. _fo11ising radiatlo11s:These_ include X-rays, gammara.ys, cosmic rays. They are highly lethal to DNA
and thus kill all types of micro-organisms. They
can penetrate solids and liquids wi thout raising the
temperature appreciably ( cold sterilisation). They are
used for sterilising plastic syringes, swabs, catheters,
tUlbings, etc.

2. _Non-ionising radiatiori:_ They act as a form of hot air
sterilization since they are absorbed as heat. They
include the infrared rays which is used for rapid mass
sterilisation of disposable syringes.

## Disinfection Of Operation Theatre

**1.** Fumigation refers to disinfection of the operating
room by exposu re to the fumes of a va porised
diisinfectant.

_Formaldehyde_ is an effective agent which has been
commonly used to sterilise the operating room. For
optimum disinfection, formaldehyde fumigation is
recommended fortnightly as a routine and at the end
of an operating session of a grossly infected case.
_**Method of fumigation**_ involves fo llowing steps:

- _Cleaning and scrubbing_ of the operating room is
done tl1oroughly.

- _Carbolisation_ of the floor is carried out after the
cleaning.

- _Sealing_ of all the apem1res in the room is done with
adhesive tape prior to fumigation leaving only one
door open.

- _Generation of formaldehyde_ is done by addition of
150 gm of potassium permanganate (KMnO to
4)
280 mL of formalin in a steel bucket for every l 000
cubic feet of room volume. Alternatively, 500 m L of
40% formaldehyde in one literof water is put into an
electric boiler or in a large bowl placed on a electric
hot plate with safety cut-out when dry on boiling.
Alternatively, formaldehyde 40% diluted in water is
spilled over the floor of the operating room.

- _Closure and sealing of the door_ is done quickly. After
formaldehyde vapour is generated the room should
be left closed for 24 to 48 hours.

- _Neutralisation of formaldehyde_ is then carried out
witll ammonium solution left in the operating room
for a few hours. One liter of ammonium solution
plus one liter of water is required to neutralize every
liter of 40% formaldehyde used.

_• Replacement of left out formalin_ _with_ _air._
Subsequently, the room doors may be opened for
a short period or tile air-conditioning be switched
on to replace tile formalin witll air.
**Note.** Presently formalization is not preferred
because ofi.ts side effects.
2. **Ultraviolet light sterilization.** Use of UV diodes
contai ning apparatus overnight in the operating
room can help in sterilization of 01'.
**3. Bacillocidresentacombinationof multipledisinfectants, is** formaldehyde free and thus does not
irritate tile mucosa] surfaces of the operating staff
tile next day.
**4.** Bacillol useful for instant disinfection, is used as a
spray. However, it does not act on the spores.

## Related Questions

## Anaesthesia For Ocular Surgery

**How topical ocular anaesthesia is achieved? Whatare its indications?**
Topical ocular anaestllesia is achieved by instillation
of2 to 4% xylocaine or l % amethocaine, 4 times every
4 minute.

_**I**_ _**ndications**_

- For minor procedures like removal of corneal
foreign body, removal of stitches, etc.

- Along with retrobulbar block.

- Recently, phacoemulsificati on operation is being
done under topical anaestllesia.

**What are various techniques of facialblock ana-esthesia?**

 - _Van Lint's block:_ Terminal branches of facial nerve
are blocked by injecting 2.5 mL of anaesthetic
solution in deeper tissues just above the eyebrows
and just below the inferior orbital margin.

_• O'Brien's block:_ Facial nerve is blocked at tile neck
of mandible.

- _Nadbath block:_ Facial nerve is blocked near the
stylomastoid foramen.

- _Atkinson's block:_ Only superior branches of facial
nerve are blocked by an injection at the inferior
margin of zygomatic bone.

**Where injection is made for retrobulbar block?**
For a retrobulbar block, 2 mL of 2% xylocaine is
injected into the muscle cone.

**What are the effects of retrobulbar block?**

- Ciliary nerve and ciJimy ganglion block

- Ocular akinesia

- Ocular anaesthesia and analgesia

- Dilatation of the pupil

- Ocular hypotony.

**Enumerate complications of retrobulbar block.**

- Retrobulbar haemorrhage

- Globe perforation

- Optic nerve injury

- Extraocular muscle palsies.

**What is the technique of peribulbar block?**
An anaestlletic solution, 6 to 9 mL (a mixture of2%
xylocaine and 0.5-0.75% bupivacaine) in a ratio of
2:1 witll hya1uronidase 5 IU/ mL with or without
adrenalin e l :1 lac is injected into the peripheral
orbital space.

**What are the advantages of peribulbar block?**

- o separate facial block is required.

- Complications associated wi.th retrobulbar block
are almost eliminated.

## Operations For Cataract Extraction

**Name theinstrumentsrequiredforthebasicintraocular surgery set.**
The instruments required include superior rectus
holding fo rceps, Stevens' needle holder, artery
forceps, plane forceps, curved-ringed scissors, heat
cautery or wet-field cautery, razor-blade fragment
ho lder, corneoscleral suturing forceps, corneoscleral
section enlarging scissors, iris forceps, deWecker's
iridectomy scissors, lens spatula, lens hook, anterior
chamber cannula, iris repositor, spring action fine
needle holder.

These instruments from the basic intraocular
surgery set.

**Name the additionalinstruments required forextra capsular cataract extraction.**
Cystitome, Vannas' scis ors, McPherson's forceps,
two-way irrigation-aspiration cannula; posterior
capsule polisher.

**Name (pick up) the additional instruments requiredfor an intraocular lens implantation.**
Anterior chamber cannula for injecting viscoelasti c
s ubstance, intraocu lar lens (implant) holding

forceps, foldable IOL injector and intraocular lens
dialer.

**What are the advantages of ECCE over ICCE?**

1. Extracapsular cataract extraction is a universal

operation and can be performed at au ages, except
when zonules are not intact While ICCE cannot
be performed below 40 years of age.
2. Posterior chamber IOL can be implanted after

ECCE, while it cannot be implanted after ICCE.
3. Postoperative vitreous-related problems (such as

herniation in anterior chamber, pupillary block
and vitreous touch syndrome) associated with
ICCE are not seen after ECCE.
4. Incidence of postopera tive complications such as

endophthalmiLis, cysLoid macular oedema and
retinal detachment is much less after ECCE as
compared to that after ICCE.

**What are the advantages of ICCE over ECCE?**

l. The technique of ICCE as compared to ECCE

is s imple, cheap, easy a nd does not need
sophisticated microinsLruments.
2. Postoperative opacification of posterior capsule is

seen in a sign ificant number of cases after ECCE.

No such problem is there after ICCE.

3. ICCE is less time consuming and hence more

useful than ECCE for mass scale operations in
eye-camps.

**Name the different techniques of extracapsularcat;nact extraction.**

l. Discission or needling (obsolete)
2. Linear extraction or cu rette evacuation (obsolete)
3. Modern extracapsular cataract extraction (ECCE)
4. Small incision cataract smgery (SICS)
s. Lensectomy
6. Phacoemulsification.

**What are the main steps of lens removal inECCEopE!ration?**

l. Anterior capsulotomy
2. Removal of nucleus
3. Aspiration of the cortical lens matter.

**Name the techniques of anterior capsulotomy.**

1. Can-opener technique
2. Linear capsulotomy (envelope technique)
3. Continuous circular capsulorhexis ( CCC).

**What is phacoemulsification?**
Phacoemulsification is a technique of extracapsular
cataract extraction in which after the removal of
anterior capsule (by capsulorhexis), the lens nucleus
is emulsified and aspirated with the probe of a
phacoemulsification machine.

**What are the advantages of phacoemulsificatlonove!r the conventional ECCE operation?**

l. Corneoscleral incision required is very small

(3 mm). Therefore, sutureless surgery is possible
with a self-sealing scleral tunnel incision.
2. Early visual rehabilitation of the patient.
3. Very less astigmatism.

**What is femtosecond laser-assisted cataract surgery(FliACS)7**
FLACS is the most recent technique of micro-incision
cataract surgery (MlCS) in which femtosecond
laser is used to perform certain steps which include
capsulotomy, primary and secondary clear corneal
incisions, astigmatic arcuate incisions and nuclear
fragmentation.

**What are advantages of FLACS over conventionalmic rophacoemulsification?**

- _Capsulotomy_ is precise, accurate and predictable.
Especially useful in mature and hypermature
ca1taracts and when premium IOLs are planned.

- _Corneal_ _incisions_ offer the advantages of
decreased leakage, added stability, accuracy, and
reproducibility.

- _Nuclear fragmentation_ with FSL, decreases the
use of ultrasound energy and instrumentation and
manipulation. This improves safety and decreases
complications.

**What are the main types of intraocular lenses (IOL)?**
The major classes of IOL based on the method of
fixation in the eye are as follows:

1. Anterior chamber IOL, e.g. 'Kelman multiplex

(IOL)'.
2. Iris suppo1ted lenses, e.g. Singh-Worst's iris claw

lens.
3. Posterior chamber IOL.

**Whenandwhoperformedthe firstsuccessfulintraocular implant operation?**
Harold Ridley, a British ophthal mologist, performed th e first IOL implantation on November
29, 1949.

**Name a few preoperative complications of cataractoperation.**

l. Injury to cornea (Desceme1's detachment)
2. Accidental rupture of the lens capsule
3. Vitreous loss
4. Expulsive choroidal haemorrhage.

**Name some early postoperative complications ofcataract extraction.**

1. Hyphaema
2. lris prolapse
3. Striate keratopathy
4. Flat (shallow) anterior chamber
5. Bacterial endophthalmitis.

**Whataredelayedcomplicationsof cataractextraction?**
1. Cystoid macular oedema (CME)
2. Retinal detachment (RD)
3. Epithelial in growth
4. After-cataract.

**What are the types of after-cataract?**
1. Thin m embranous after-cataract (thickened

posterior capsule).
2. Dense membranous after-cataract
3. Soemmerri.ng's ring after-cataract
4. Elschnig's pearls.

**Name the IOL-related complications.**

l. Malpositions of the JOL, e.g. inferior subluxation

(Sunset syndrome), superior subluxation (Sunrise
syndrome), dislocation of IOL in the vitreous
cavity (lost lens syndrome).
2. Toxic lens syndrome (IOL-induced iritis).

**What are the advantages of an IOL implantation overspectacle correction of aphakia?**
1. 10 maginification of the object.
2. No prob Lem of anisometropia in wliocular aphakia.
3. Elimina1tion of aberrations and prismatic effect of

thick glaisses.
4. Wider and better field of vision.
5. Cosmetically more acceptable.

**What is pc,stoperative management of cataractoperation?**

1. The patiient is asked to lie quietly upon his/ her

back for about one hour and advised to take nil
orally.
2. For mild-to-mode rate postoperative pain,

injectioin diclofenac sodium (Voveran) may be
given intramuscularly.
3. In the morn ing after about 24 hours of operation,

bandag,e is removed and the eye is inspected
thoroughly fo r any postoperative complication.
Under normal circumstances, eye is opened with
the use of one drop of I% cyclopentolate, one drop
of antibiotic and steroid drops and tinted glasses
are advised.
4. Antibiotic steroid eye drops are continued for fo ur

times, three Limes, two times and then once a day
for 2 weeks each.
5. Final spectacles are prescribed after about8 weeks

ofoperation.

**What do y01u understand by primary and secondaryIOL implantation?**
Primary IOL implantation refers to the use of IOL
during surgery for cataract, while secondary IOL
is implanted to correct aphakia in a previo usly
operated eye.

**How williroucalculate the power of posteriorchamber IC)L to be implanted?**
The powe r of the TOL to be implanted can be
calculated iusing keratometry and A-scan ultrasound.
SRK formula commonly employed to calculate IOL
power is as: fo llows:

P = A-2.5L--0.9K; where P = IOL power in dioptres,
A= specific: conslant of the IOL, L= axial length of the
eyeball in mm and **K=** average keratometric reading.

**IRIDEClfOMY OPERATIONWhat is iriclectomy and what are its types?**
Iridectomy is an abscission of a part of the iris. It is
of the following types:

- Peripheral iridectomy

- Key-hole iridectomy

- Broad or sector iridectomy.

**632** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

**What are the indications of iridectomy operation?**
1. Abscission of the prolapsed iris
2. For optical purposes (optical irideccomy)
3. As a part of glaucoma operation
4. For removal of foreign body, cyst or tumour of the

iris.

**What is iridotomy and how is it performed?**
Iridotomy means just incising a pan of the iris. It can
be performed by two methods:

    - Surgical iridotomy

    - Laser iridotomy.

**What are indications of iridotomy operation?**
1. Laser iridotomy for primary narrow-angle glaucoma
2. Four-dot iridotomy for iris bombe.

## Surgical Procedures For Glaucoma

**Name the various surgical procedures for glaucoma.**
1. Peripheral iridectomy
2. Goniotomy
3. TrabecuJotomy
4. Filtration operations
5. Seton operaLion (glaucoma valve operation)
6. Cycloablative procedures.

**Peripheral iridectomy operation is performed forwhich type of glaucoma?**

    - Primary angle closure suspect (PACS) with positive
provocative test.

    - PAC suspect in fellow eye of patient with PAC
glaucoma in other eye.

    - Primary angle closure (manifest).

    - Primary angle closure glaucoma.

**What are filtration operations for glaucoma?**
In filtration operations, passage is made for
the drainage of aqueous humour into the subconjunctival space. Trabeculectomy is presently
the most frequently performed filtration surgery.
Other filtration operations which are not performed
now included: Elliot's sclerocorneaJ trephining,
punch sclerectomy, iridencleisis, Scheie's thermal
sclerostomy and cyclod,ialys~~

**What aretheindicationsof trabeculectomyoperation?**
1. Primary angle-closure glaucoma with peripheral

anterior synechiae involving more than half of tl1e
angle.
2. Primary open-angle glaucoma.
3. Congenital and development glaucoma where

trabeculotomy and goniotomy fail.
4. Selective cases of secondary open angle glaucoma

and secondary narrow-angle glaucoma.

**What are the advantages of trabeculectomy overother filtration operations?**
1. Incidence of postoperative shallow or flat anterior

chamber is very less.
2. Incidence of postoperative hypolony is very low.
3. Chances of postoperative infection through the

filtration bleb are low.
4. Quality of filtration bleb formed is good.

**What is Seton operation?**
In this operation, a valvular synthetic tube is
implanted which drains the aqueous humour from
the anterior chamber into tl1e subconjw1ctival space.

It is performed for neovascular glaucoma aind
intractable cases of primary and other secondary
glaucomas where medical treatment a nd
conventional filtration surgery fail.

**What are cycloablative procedures?**
In these procedures, ciliary epithelium is destroyed
to control the intraocuJar pressure. These procedures
are used for absolute glaucoma. Commonly employed
cycloablative procedures include: cyclocryope·xy,
cyclophotocoagulation and cyclodiathenny.

**Cyclodialysis operation isuseful in which typE! ofglaucoma?**

- Glaucoma in aphakes.

## Enucleation And Evisceration

## Operations

**What is enucleation operation?**
It is complete excision of the eyeball.

**Enumerate indications of enucleation.**
_**Absolute indica.tions are:**_

- Retinoblastoma

- Malignant melanoma.
_**llelatiue indications are:**_

- Painful blind eye

- Mutilating ocular injury

- Phthisis bulbi

- Anterior staphyloma.
_Indication for eye donation._ Removal of eyes from the
cadaver is presentJy the most common indication for
enucleation.

**What precautions should be taken while perform1ingenucleation in a patient with retinoblastoma?**
During enucleation, the longest possible piece- of
optic nerve should be excised.

**What is evisceration operation?**
It is removal of the contents of the eyeball leaving
behind the sclera. Frill evisceration is preferred over
simple evisceration. In this, only about 3 mm frill of
the sclera is left around the optic nerve.

**What are the indications of evisceration?**

- Panophthalmitis

- fa'Pttlsive choroidaJ haemorrhage

- Bleeding anterior staphyloma.

**How can the cosmetic appearance be improvedafter enucleation or evisceration operation?**
For bes t res u lts, an orbital im pl an l sho uld be
implanted at the time of surgery and an arti ficial
eye of plastic should be worn after about 2 weeks of
surgery. A delay in the use of artificial eye may lead
to a contracted socket.

## Operations On Eyelids

For important questions related tO eyelid operations

_See_ pages 573-577.

## Lacrimal Apparatus Operations

Ques tions related to operations on the lacrimal

apparatus are described on pages 577-579.

## Lasers And Cryotherapy In

## Ophthalmology

**What are the properties of laser light?**

- Monochromaticity

- Coherence

- Collimation.

**Name the different types of lasers and theirmechanism of action.**

_Type of laser_ _Mechanism of action_
Argon Photocoagulation
Krypton Photocoagulation
Diode Photocoagulation
Frequ ency doubled
Nd-YAG Photocoagulation
Nd-YAG Photodisruption
Excimer Photoablation

**Enumerate uses of argon/diode laser.**
_**In glaucoma:**_

1. Laser trabeculoplasty for primary open-angle

glaucoma.
2. La se r go n io pun c tur e for d eve lopm e n tal

glau coma.
3. Laser iridotomy for narrow-angle glaucoma.
4. Cyclophotocoagulation for absolute glaucoma.

_**In lesio11S of reti11a:**_

1. Diabe tic retinopathy
2. Eales' disease
3. Coats' disease
4. Sickle-cell retinopathy
5. Exudative age-related m acular degeneration.

**What are the therapeutic uses of Nd-YAG laser?**

l. Capsttl o,tomy for thickened posterior capsul e.
2. Me mbranectomy for pupiJJary me mbranes.

**What are therapeutic applications of excimer laser?**

1. LAS IK for correction of refractive errors.
2. Photo refractive keratectomy ( PRK) for correction

of myopia and hyperm etropia.
3. Photolherapeutic keratectomy (PTK) for corneal

d iseases such as band-shaped keratopathy.

**Describe the laser treatment for diabeticretinopathy.**
Photocoagulation by frequency doubled d-YAG
laser (532 nm) is e mployed as follows:
1. _Panretir.talphotocoagulatiorz_ (PRP) is indicated in

severe cases of prepro LiJeraUve a nd proliferative
diabetic rctinopathy.
2. _Focal Laser burns_ are applied in the centre of the

hard exudate's ring in focal ex:udative macuJopathy.
3. _Grid pallern laser burns_ are a pplied in diffuse

exudativc maculopathy.

**What is femtosecond laser? Enumerate its applic-ations.**
A femtosecond laser uses an in frared beam of light

to precisely sepa rate the tissues through a process
called pho ro-disrup tion by generating pulses as
short as one-qu adrillionth of a second ( 10 [15 ] =
femtoseco nd ). It has a wavelength of 1053 run a nd
is based upon the technology whereby focu sed laser
pulses divide material at the molecular level without
transfer oflheat or impact to the surrounding tissue.
_**Uses of Jemtosecoml laser**_ include:

- Creation nf a corneal flap for **LASIK**

- Crea tion of corn e al tunn e ls fo r impl a nti ng
intracorn,eal ring implants

- Arcuate incisions to correct astigmalism

- Corneal keratoplasty incisions

- Femtose,cond laser assisted ca ta ract su rge ry
(FLACS).

**What do yo,u mean by cryopexy?**
Cryop exy m eans to p rod uce ti ss ue injury by
applicatioT11 of extremely low te mperalure (-100° C
to -40° C). This is achieved by a cryoprobe from a
cryo-unit.

**On what principle is the working of a cryoprobebased?**
Working of a cryoprobe is based on the Joule Thompson princiiple of cooling.

**Which gas** i1s **used in a cryo-machine?**
The cryoun it u ses freon, nitrous oxide or carb on
dioxide gas as a cooli ng agent.

**634** SECTION VI PRACTICAL OPHTHALMOLOGY

**Enumerate the applications of cryo in ophthalmology.**
l. _Lids:_ (i) cryolysis for trichiasis, (ii) cryoth erapy for

warts and molluscum contagiosum, (iii) cryotherapy fo r basal cell carcinoma and haemangioma.
2. _Conjunctiva:_ Cr yo therapy for hypertrophied

papillae of vernal catarrh.
3. _Lens:_ Cryoextraction of the cataractous lens by

TCCE technique was done in olden days (not done
nowaday).

4. _Ci/iary body:_ Cyclocryopexy for absolute glaucoma

and neovascular glaucoma.
5. _Retina:_ (i) Cryopexy is widely used fo r sealing

retinal breaks in reti na l d etac hme nt, (i i)
Prophylactic cryopexy to prevent retinal detachment in certain prone cases, (iii) Anterior retinal
cryopexy (ARC) fo r neovascularization and, (iv)
Cryotreatment of retinoblastoma.
